The suffix وا indicates 'they' (3rd person masculine plural) as the subject

steps.m: if (introduces a hypothetical condition in شَآءَ 'willed')
steps.n: This is a conditional word that introduces an imaginary or hypothetical situation. In this ayah, it connects to the verb شَآءَ (willed) to express 'if Allah had willed...'

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: Added the vowels 'a' after each letter to make it mean 'he wanted' in the past
The hamza changes to a weak letter due to Arabic spelling rules
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added a long vowel mark (small alif) after the second letter to extend the sound
Added 'the' to make it refer to a specific deity
Added U mark at end to show this word is the subject

This is a negative particle that makes the verb عَبَدْنَا (we worshipped) into 'we did not worship'. In this ayah, the disbelievers are saying they would not have worshipped other than Allah if He had willed.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action in the past. The sukun (ْ) on د helps connect it to نَا smoothly.
This is a preposition that shows where something starts from, what it's made of, or which group it belongs to. Here it connects to دُونِهِۦ (besides Him) to show what they worshipped besides Allah.

steps.m: to be below or less than something

steps.n: Adding damma (ُ) changes it to mean 'below/lesser'
Adding kasra shows this word connects to the next word
Added هِ to show this refers back to Allah mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: from/of (shows where something starts or what it's made of)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows the starting point or origin of something. In this ayah, it's used with دُونِهِۦ (besides Him) to mean 'from besides Him'
Root letters joined together with vowels to make the word for 'thing'
The hamza at the end changes to hamza on the line because it comes after a sukoon
The ي changes to ى (alif maqsurah) in the written form while keeping the same pronunciation
Added two dots under (tanween) to show it's 'of/from a thing' when used after مِن
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'we' that doesn't come from a root. The ُ (damma) at the end shows it's doing the action in the sentence.

The ّ (shadda) on the first letter adds emphasis, like saying 'we ourselves' or stressing 'WE' when speaking. This emphasis connects to how the speakers in this verse are strongly asserting their position.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer

steps.m: to care for as a father

steps.n: Root letters أ-ب-و become the simple word for 'father'
Changes from 'father' to 'fathers' by adding extra letters and sounds - a broken plural form
Combines alifs for writing style
Changes ending to show this word is doing the action (adds a waaw with damma)
Adds 'our' meaning by adding نَا at the end. This word is part of the subject of the verb عَبَدْنَا (we worshipped).
This is a basic negation word that means 'not' or 'no'. In this ayah, it's used with the verb حَرَّمْنَا to say 'we did not forbid'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. Here it connects 'we did not worship' with 'we did not forbid'
The doubled ر with shadda shows this is a strong/intense prohibition, not just a simple one

steps.m: we made (something) completely forbidden (referring to what the polytheists falsely claimed about Allah's prohibitions)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. In this ayah, this refers to what the polytheists falsely claimed about making things forbidden without Allah's permission.
This is a basic preposition showing source, origin, or connection. In this ayah, it connects to the word شَىْءٍ (thing) to show what they didn't worship.

steps.m: to be lower, beneath, or less than
steps.n: This word indicates something lesser or excluding something
Adding damma makes it into a word meaning 'lesser than' or 'without'
Added kasra to connect it to the next word
Added هِۦ to show this refers to Allah (him). This word is the object of عَبَدْنَا (we worshipped) in this ayah.

This is a basic preposition that connects words by showing where something starts, what it's made of, or which group it belongs to. Here it connects to the word شَىْءٍ (thing) to say 'of/from any thing'
Connected the root letters and added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter. The hamza (ء) replaces the root letter (أ) at the end due to spelling rules.
The letter ي is written as ى when it has a sukun. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect meaning.
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end because this word comes after مِن (from/of) which requires the genitive case.
Starting with the comparison particle ك that shows similarity
Added ذٰ (that) to specify what is being compared to
Added لِ to make it point to something specific (like saying 'exactly like that')
Added كَ at the end which means 'you/your', showing someone is being spoken to. Works with فَعَلَ (did) to say 'like that you did'
Basic root meaning showing the concept of doing or acting

steps.m: he did (referring to what was done by those before them)
steps.n: Added fathas to create past tense verb. In this ayah, it refers to what previous generations did, connecting to الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ (those before them).
Relative pronoun used for plural masculine. The lām has shadda due to sun letter assimilation with ذ.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it's used with دُونِهِۦ (besides Him) to emphasize what came before.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun meaning 'before'
Added kasra to show this word is connected to what comes after it
Added هِمْ at the end to show this means 'before them'

steps.m: A word used to ask yes/no questions
steps.n: This is a question particle that always comes at the start of a sentence to ask yes/no questions

steps.m: So, is it that...? (connecting to عَلَى ٱلرُّسُلِ)
steps.n: The فَ adds the meaning of 'so' or 'then' to connect this question with what came before it. This question introduces the next phrase عَلَى ٱلرُّسُلِ (upon the messengers).
This is a preposition that shows something is required of or expected from the messengers in this context
This forms the plural of رَسُول (messenger)
The و makes it mean 'one who does the action a lot' - so a messenger is someone who delivers many messages
Changed the vowels to make it plural - this is called a 'broken plural' because it changes the inside of the word instead of adding something at the end
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific and doubled the ر with a shadda
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word على (upon) in the ayah

steps.m: except (what comes after it from what came before it)
steps.n: This is a basic word that shows something is being excluded. In this ayah, it connects 'clear delivery of the message' as the only duty of the messengers.
Added basic vowel marks to connect the letters
Added alif after second letter to make it into a noun meaning the result of conveying
Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show it's the subject. The alif is written as ٰ when it comes after ل.
This is the basic concept of making something clear or distinct
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
The مُ at the start and ِ in the middle make this describe someone or something that does the action of making clear
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ُ to show it's describing the message mentioned before
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of the base word قد which shows certainty
Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis to the certainty
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: to send forth or raise up

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: we sent forth (connected to رَّسُولًا - a messenger)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word connects to رَّسُولًا (a messenger) - showing that 'we sent forth a messenger'

steps.m: in (location within كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ - every nation)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it shows that the messengers were sent within or to every nation.
The doubled ل (shadda) shows completeness - meaning every single one with no exceptions. The damma (ُ) makes it into a noun.
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word comes after فِى (in) in the phrase 'in every nation'

steps.m: to be in front, to lead as an example

steps.n: Added damma and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning the source or origin of something
Adding ة changes it from 'mother/source' to mean a whole community or nation of people
Added tanween kasra to show this word is possessed by كُلِّ (every) in the phrase كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ (every nation)

steps.m: to send or deliver a message

steps.n: The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role

steps.m: a messenger (object of بَعَثْنَا - we sent)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this word is receiving the action of being sent. This messenger is being sent by Allah.
This is a connecting word that introduces what the messenger (رَّسُولًا) commanded. It's like putting quotation marks before someone's words in English.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he worshipped' to a command 'worship!' by adding U sound at start and putting sukoon at end
Changed to address multiple people by adding وا at the end and changing the first letter to ٱ
Changed to the unique name for the One True God, with shadda on ل to emphasize its special status

steps.m: Allah (as the object being worshipped in اعْبُدُوا)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being worshipped. The word connects to اعْبُدُوا (worship) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changed to 'I' form with ا and ت added to show 'I keep myself away'
Changed from 'I' form to 'you all' command form

steps.m: and keep away! (all of you) (command to avoid ٱلطَّاغُوتَ)
steps.n: Added 'wa' (وَ) meaning 'and' at the start + command to a group to stay away

steps.m: to cross boundaries, go beyond limits
false god or ruler who crosses all limits
steps.n: Root letters plus او and ت to make a noun meaning someone/something that goes way beyond proper limits
Added و after second root letter غ and changed final ي to ت to create noun form indicating one who embodies transgression
Added ال at the beginning to show we're talking about a specific false god/ruler

steps.m: the false god/ruler (being avoided in اجْتَنِبُوا)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this is what's being avoided in the verb اجْتَنِبُوا (avoid/stay away from)
Basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to. Here it connects to مَّنْ (who/whoever) to show we're talking about different groups from among the people.

steps.m: from them/among them (referring to the nations mentioned in أُمَّةٍ)
steps.n: Added هُم which means 'them', referring back to the nations (أُمَّةٍ) mentioned earlier who received messengers

steps.m: so from them (introducing the two groups: those guided and those who deserved misguidance)
steps.n: Added فَ meaning 'so/then' to show this introduces the result or consequence - here it introduces how these nations were divided into two groups: those who were guided and those who deserved misguidance
Basic form of the word that asks about or refers to people. In this ayah, it's used to divide people into two groups - those guided and those not guided.
Added sukun at the end to show the word stops here
Added shadda because this word comes after a word ending in ن (مِنْهُم), causing the م to be emphasized in pronunciation

steps.m: to guide to the right path

steps.n: Root letters got vowel marks (fathas) to make past tense verb meaning 'he guided'
Changed final ي to ى since this is a weak-ending verb
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Preposition with attached pronoun
Added conjunction و meaning 'and'
This is a basic word that asks about or refers to people. It's like saying 'who' or 'whoever' in English.

steps.m: who/whoever (refers to some people from مِنْهُم)
steps.n: Added emphasis mark (shadda) because it comes after another م in مِنْهُم. In Arabic, when the same letter appears twice in a row, they often combine with a shadda. Here it refers back to some of the people mentioned (مِنْهُم).
Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter to show something is definitely true

steps.m: she/it became firmly established (subject of الضَّلٰلَةُ)
steps.n: Added ت at the end to show that الضَّلٰلَةُ (misguidance) is what became established
This is a basic preposition showing something being on top of or upon something else
The ى changes to ي with sukun when adding a suffix

steps.m: upon him (referring to the path of misguidance mentioned in الضَّلٰلَةُ)
steps.n: Adding هِ (hi) to عَلَيْ (upon) makes it mean 'upon him' - like adding 'him' to show who is affected
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
Added ا after second letter to form noun pattern فعال indicating continuous action. This word is in nominative case as it is the subject of حقت (became deserved/established).
Added ة at the end to make the noun feminine, which is common for abstract concepts
Added ال to make it specific/definite

steps.m: the state of being lost/astray (subject of حَقَّتْ)
steps.n: Added damma (u-sound) at the end because it's doing the action in the sentence
Added kasra to make it into a command form
Added وا to make it a command for multiple people
Added فَ to connect this command with what came before it in the verse

steps.m: in (location within كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ - every nation)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it shows the location where messengers were sent - within every nation.

steps.m: in (location within كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ - every nation)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'fi'

steps.m: in (location within كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ - every nation)
steps.n: Changed the final ي to ى which is a common spelling variation at the end of words. Both are pronounced the same way.

steps.m: to be wide and spread out
steps.n: The root refers to something being expansive or spread out
Root letters were given vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to make the noun meaning 'earth/land'
Added kasra (i sound) at the end because it comes after the word 'in' - this shows connection between the words
Added ال with hamzat wasl (ٱ) to make it specific. This word follows فِى (in) in فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (so travel in the earth)
Basic meaning of seeing or observing
Added vowels to connect the letters into a past tense verb
Changed from 'he looked' to a command meaning 'look!' by adding 'u' at start and changing the ending
Added وا to make it plural command
Added فَ at beginning to connect it with previous statement, showing sequence
This is a question word used to ask about the way or manner something happens
Adding vowel marks to show exactly how to pronounce this question word

steps.m: how/in what manner (asking about عٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ - the end of the deniers)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is being used as a question word about the state/manner of something specific in the sentence
Basic meaning of something existing or happening
Added vowel marks (fathas) to change from the root meaning 'to be' into 'he was' in the past tense

steps.m: he was/existed/happened (describing the end result of ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in a past tense verb. This is a common change with weak letters to make pronunciation easier.

steps.m: to follow or come after something
steps.n: These letters together represent the basic idea of something following or coming after something else
Root means 'follow/come after' - adding ة makes it mean 'the final outcome that follows'
Changed alif to alif with bar (ٰ) and added damma to show this is the subject of كَانَ (was)

steps.m: to deny truth, to reject as false
to strongly reject/deny something as false
steps.n: Doubling the middle letter shows doing something with more strength or repeatedly
Changed from describing the action to describing the person who does it
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people. This word relates to عاقبة (end/outcome) in the ayah, showing what happened to these deniers.

steps.m: the deniers (referring to specific groups who denied their messengers)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to specific known groups - in this case, the people mentioned in the ayah who rejected their messengers

steps.m: if (introduces condition about trying to guide them)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects to تَحْرِصْ (you try) to say 'if you try' to guide them.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning

steps.m: you are eager (addressing Prophet Muhammad ﷺ about هُدَىٰهُمْ/their guidance)
steps.n: The ت at the beginning shows 'you' are doing the action. The sukun at the end shows this is in a special form because it comes after إِن (if/even if). This is addressing Prophet Muhammad ﷺ about his concern for people's guidance.
This is the basic form of the word that means something being high or elevated
Adding ى changes this from a concept of height into a word that connects things, showing one thing is 'upon' or 'on' another. Here it connects to هُدَىٰهُمْ (their guidance)
The small alif (ٰ) is added as a pronunciation helper to show the long 'aa' sound at the end
Added damma and fatha to create the noun form meaning 'guidance'
The ي changes to ى with an alif above (ٰ) because it's at the end of the word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هُمْ at the end to show the guidance belongs to them
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it definite and adds certainty to the statement

steps.m: then indeed/certainly (connects to إِن تَحْرِصْ before it)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this statement with the condition mentioned before it ("if you desire their guidance")
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' or 'something worshipped'
Added ال (the) and merged it with the word, then added emphasis (shadda) to show this is THE one true deity. The hamza at the start changed to alif with wasl mark.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يَهْدِى - guides)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon by the verb يَهْدِى (guides)
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it mean the opposite. In this ayah, it makes 'guides' into 'does not guide'.
Added fatha mark on ل to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'
Added vowels to connect the letters. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.

steps.m: he guides (subject of لا in لا يَهْدِى)
steps.n: Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense. The middle letter gets a sukun (ْ) and the last letter gets a kasra (ِ) in present tense. This word is negated by لا (does not) in the ayah.
Added ى at the end which is a common way to write the long vowel sound in present tense verbs
This is a special word used to talk about any person in general. In this ayah, it refers to whoever Allah leads astray.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding shadda (doubling) on the ل changes it from 'going astray' to 'making others go astray'
Adding alif at start makes it mean 'to cause someone else to do the action'

steps.m: he leads astray (subject of لا يَهْدِى)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This word relates to لا يَهْدِى (does not guide) in the verse, showing the contrast between guiding and misguiding.
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before. In this ayah, it negates the word نَّاصِرِينَ (helpers), saying there are no helpers for them.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation when connecting to next part

steps.m: for them (referring to those who are led astray in يُضِلُّ)
steps.n: Added هُم which means 'them' (masculine plural). This word connects to يُضِلُّ (leads astray) in the ayah, showing who has no helpers.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'from' or shows where something starts
The م is doubled (has a shadda) because it comes after a word ending with ن or م, making it easier to pronounce when connected
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the pattern to show someone who helps
Added emphasis mark (shadda) on first letter
Added ين to make it plural. The word comes after the preposition مِن (from/of) which is why it ends this way.
Added a small diagonal line (fatHa) at the end because it follows the word مِن (min)
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes the swearing more emphatic/stronger
وا shows that multiple people did the action
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added shadda on ل to emphasize this is the one true God, not just any deity
Added fatha on the shadda for proper pronunciation
Changed initial alif to wasl form since it connects with previous letter

steps.m: by/with Allah (object of وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ - they swore)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to mean 'by/with' and kasra at end because it's the object of 'they swore'

steps.m: utmost effort (object of أَقْسَمُوا - they swore)
steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'utmost effort'. This word is what they swore by, along with Allah's name, in their oath.
Added vowel marks to form the singular noun for 'oath', which comes from the practice of taking oaths with the right hand
Plural form made by adding alif at start and changing middle vowels - like many Arabic plurals
Changed ending to show this word is possessed by something after it
Added pronoun suffix to show who the oaths belong to
This is a basic negative word that makes the verb after it negative. Here it makes 'raises' into 'does not raise'.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Added fathas to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning
Changed to present tense by: 1) adding يَ at start, 2) putting sukun on ب, 3) adding damma at end. This word is part of Allah's promise (وَعْدًا) in this ayah.
Added shadda on lam to emphasize this is the one supreme deity

steps.m: Allah (The One True God) (subject of يَبْعَثُ - raises)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action of raising (يَبْعَثُ) in this ayah. The initial alif became a wasl alif since it connects with the previous word.
This is the basic form of the word that asks about or refers to people. In this ayah, it refers to the people who die (يَمُوتُ).
Added fatha mark to show proper pronunciation. This word connects to يَمُوتُ (dies) in the ayah, referring to those who die.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed vowel pattern for hollow verb (middle weak letter و)
Added present tense prefix يَ and changed final vowel to ُ to show it's the subject. This word is part of what the disbelievers swore, saying 'Allah will not resurrect whoever dies'
This is a particle that contradicts negative statements. Added vowel marks to show how it's pronounced.

steps.m: yes indeed (contradicting the negative statement لَا يَبْعَثُ)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to make it stronger and more emphatic. This word specifically contradicts the previous negative statement that 'Allah will not resurrect those who die'.

steps.m: yes indeed, certainly (emphatically contradicting the negative statement لَا يَبْعَثُ)
steps.n: Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced as a long vowel. This is just a pronunciation helper.
Changed from the root meaning 'to promise' into the noun 'a promise' by adding vowel marks (fatha and sukun)
Added an alif and tanween (double damma) at the end to show this is a direct object - it tells us what they promised
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. It's one of the common prepositions in Arabic that doesn't come from a root.
When adding a pronoun after على, the ى changes to يْ to make it easier to connect with the next part
Added هِ at the end which means 'him' or 'it'. This shows who or what is being referred to.
Added shadda to strengthen the meaning and make it a noun. This word appears in وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا (a promise upon Him, truly) where it emphasizes that the promise is true.
Adding tanween alif (ًا) makes it describe how something is - in this case, it emphasizes that Allah's promise is definitely true
This is the base particle meaning 'but'. The alif after ل is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this case.
Added shadda and fatha at the end to make the word stronger and more emphatic

steps.m: and yet/but still (connecting to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with the previous one, showing contrast between them
The vowels َُ show this is about becoming characterized by abundance
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'more' or 'most' of something

steps.m: to be social or friendly with others
people (those who are social by nature)
steps.n: Root means 'to be social' - changed to mean 'people' by adding ا in the middle
Added emphasis by doubling the ن
Added ٱلْ to specify these particular people

steps.m: of the people (possessed by أَكْثَرَ - most)
steps.n: Added ِ to show this word belongs to أَكْثَرَ (most), making the phrase 'most of the people'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَبْعَثُ (resurrect) into 'does not resurrect'. It's like adding 'not' in English.
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding ي at the start changes it from past tense (he knew) to present tense (he knows)

steps.m: they know (referring to most people in أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this refers back to 'most people' (أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ) in the ayah

steps.m: to make something clear or distinct

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels and added shadda to strengthen the meaning
Added يُ at the beginning to show this happens in the present/future

steps.m: so that he makes clear (purpose of previous action)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show this is the purpose or reason for something mentioned before
Added fatha to show the vowel sound
Added هُ (him/it) to show who the action is for

steps.m: for them (subject of يَخْتَلِفُونَ - they differ)
steps.n: Added مُ to change 'him' to 'them'. This word connects to يَخْتَلِفُونَ (they differ) by showing who is doing the differing.
Added kasra vowel mark to show basic form
Added ال to make it definite, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'

steps.m: the one who/which (subject of يَخْتَلِفُونَ - they differ)
steps.n: Changed spelling to match Uthmani script with wasl alif (ٱ) and shadda (ّ). This word connects to يَخْتَلِفُونَ to show what they differ about.

steps.m: to come after or differ from something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and ت after first letter to show people disagreeing with each other
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added kasra vowel mark to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: in it (referring to what they differ about)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ه with kasra to show this means 'in it'. The 'it' refers back to what was mentioned in the previous part of the ayah about what they differ in.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Adding يَ at the start and changing vowels makes it present/future tense. The sukun (ْ) on ل and fatha (َ) at the end are needed because this verb will be affected by particles added later.

steps.m: so that he knows (purpose of لِيُبَيِّنَ - so that he makes clear)
steps.n: Adding لِ shows this action happens for a purpose. This verb explains the purpose of the previous verb لِيُبَيِّنَ (so that he makes clear).

steps.m: and so that he knows (purpose of لِيُبَيِّنَ - so that he makes clear)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this purpose with the previous purpose mentioned
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people instead of just one person
Added ال at the beginning to point to specific people being talked about
Added final vowel mark to show this is the subject of the verb كَفَرُوٓا۟ (they disbelieved). Also changed the spelling of ال to ٱ with a waṣla, which is just a different way of writing it.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
وا shows multiple people did this in the past
The ا at the end makes the sound longer
Added shadda and fatha to emphasize the meaning
Added هُمْ at the end to show this refers to 'they/them'. This connects to the word كٰذِبِينَ (liars) in the ayah, showing that they are the ones being described as liars.
Added the basic past tense pattern by putting fathas after each letter
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a special kind of verb with a weak middle letter

steps.m: they were (referring to those who disbelieved)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to change 'he' to 'they'. This word refers back to الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ (those who disbelieved) in the ayah.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Makes the word plural and shows it's affected by كَانُوا (they were). The ين ending shows both that it's plural and that it's being acted upon by كَانُوا.
Adding shadda makes the word stronger, changing it from 'if' to 'indeed/certainly'
Adding ما changes the meaning from 'indeed' to 'only'. This word restricts the meaning of قَوْلُنَا (our word/saying) in this ayah.
Changed from action to noun - now means the thing itself (the speech) rather than the action of speaking
Added ُ (damma) to show this word is doing something in the sentence - it's the subject after إِنَّمَا
Added نَا at the end to show this is our saying. The word is the subject after إِنَّمَا (indeed)
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun meaning 'thing'
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this is an indefinite noun being acted upon by something

steps.m: to/for a thing (connected to قَوْلُنَا - our word)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show this thing is being directed towards or purposed for something. This word connects to قَوْلُنَا (our word) in the ayah.
Basic conditional particle that introduces a time or condition
Added elongated alif (آ) at the end to add emphasis and show this introduces an important condition. In this ayah, it introduces what happens when Allah wants something to happen.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at start makes it mean 'to make something happen' or 'to intend to do something'
Adding نَا at the end changes it from 'he' to 'we'
The نا was already there - it wasn't added. The only change is the final alif is written differently (with a dagger alif ٰ). Same meaning.
Added هُ at the end to show what was wanted (it)
This is a particle that connects two parts of speech, specifically introducing what will be said. In this ayah, it connects to the verb نَّقُولَ (we say) to show what will be said.
Changed to present tense base form. The middle ا returns to و and we add damma marks.
Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, and changed the ending to fatha because it comes after أَن in the ayah

steps.m: we say (after أَن in the ayah)
steps.n: The ن gets doubled (shadda) because it comes after أَن in the ayah, which affects how we say it
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced
Added the pronoun هُ which means 'him' or 'it'. This word connects to كُن (be) in this ayah, showing what is being addressed.
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

Changed to a command form by: 1) Adding damma on first letter, 2) Dropping the middle weak letter و, and 3) Making the last letter silent. This word is what Allah says when commanding something to exist, as mentioned in قَوْلُنَا (our word/saying).

steps.m: to come into being, to happen

steps.n: Basic command form telling something to exist (this is what Allah says in the ayah: كُن)
Adding ي at start makes it 'he' form in present tense
Adding ُ at end shows this is in regular case (not commanded or affected by anything)
فَ connects this word to what came before it, showing sequence or result
Starting with the basic demonstrative word that points to things
Adding ال and ي changes it from pointing at things to connecting descriptions to things or people
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people or things instead of just one
Adding و at the start connects this to what came before. This word introduces the people who will migrate (هَاجَرُوا) in Allah's cause.

steps.m: to leave or move away from something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif changes it from just 'leaving' to specifically 'migrating'

steps.m: they emigrated (referring to those who left Mecca for Allah's cause)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. Here it shows they will be settled in this world.
Added kasra (ِ) vowel mark to show how the word is pronounced
Changed the final ي to ى which is a common spelling variation at the end of words
Changed to the unique form used only for Allah, with ال merged into the word itself
Added shadda on ل to emphasize it, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added kasra at end to show possession
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects being wronged (ظُلِمُوا۟) with after (بَعْدِ) to show when something happened.
Added a small ن (called tanwin) after the main ن to help with smooth pronunciation when connecting to the next word
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukoon on second letter to make it a noun meaning 'after'
Added kasra at the end because it comes after مِنۢ (from/after) in the ayah
This is a special connecting word (relative pronoun) that links to the verb ظُلِمُوا۟ (they were wronged). It helps explain what happened after people migrated.

steps.m: to wrong, oppress, or treat unjustly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the vowels to show that someone received the action instead of doing it

steps.m: they were wronged (referring to those who migrated)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people were wronged. This refers back to الَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا (those who migrated) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: to settle or establish in a place

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes it mean 'to make someone do the action' instead of just doing it
Changed to present tense 'we' form with نُ prefix and vowel changes

steps.m: we will certainly cause to settle
steps.n: Added لَ for emphasis and future tense

steps.m: we will most certainly cause to settle
steps.n: Added نَّ for extra emphasis, and changed hamza spelling

steps.m: we will most certainly settle them
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show who is being settled
This is a basic preposition that shows something happens within or for something else. Here it connects the migration (هَاجَرُوا۟) with Allah (ٱللَّهِ) to show they migrated for Allah's sake.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed the root into a feminine noun meaning 'worldly life' by adding special vowel marks and ي. Comes from the idea of being 'near' to us (unlike the afterlife)
Added ال to make it specific
Added fatha to show it's the object of a verb
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Adds ة at the end to make it mean 'one good thing' instead of 'was good'

steps.m: a good thing/deed (object of لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ - we will surely settle them)
steps.n: Adds -an ending to show this good deed is being acted upon

steps.m: to compensate or give a reward

steps.n: Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning the reward itself
Added لَ at start to emphasize that this is definitely/surely a reward
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the word أَكْبَرُ (greater/bigger) in this ayah

steps.m: and surely the reward (subject of أَكْبَرُ)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: to come after, to be later

steps.n: Changes root to mean 'someone/something that comes after' by adding ا between first two letters
Adding ة changes the meaning from 'later' to specifically mean 'the afterlife'

steps.m: the afterlife (as opposed to worldly life)
steps.n: Adds definite article to specify this refers to the specific concept of the afterlife, contrasting with الدنيا (worldly life) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Shows this word follows أَجْرُ (reward) in the ayah, indicating the reward is of/in the afterlife
Added أ at the beginning to show comparison between things, like saying something is 'bigger' or 'greater' than something else

steps.m: larger/greater (subject of reward in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this word is the subject (the thing doing the action) in this part of the ayah
This is a conditional particle used to talk about hypothetical situations
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. In this ayah, لَوْ expresses a wish - 'if only they knew', showing regret about their lack of knowledge.

steps.m: to be or exist in a certain state or condition

steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). This is a common pattern in Arabic.
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action. This word is connected to يَعْلَمُونَ (they know) in the next ayah, showing who was doing the knowing.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed to plural by adding ونَ at the end
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things, like saying 'the one who' in English
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'. The shadda (ّ) makes the ذ sound stronger.
Adding ن at the end makes it plural, changing from 'the one who' to 'those who'. This word connects to صَبَرُوا۟ (they were patient) by describing who was patient.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word describes what the people mentioned in الَّذِينَ (those who) did.
The وا at the end shows that many people did this action together. These are the same people mentioned by الَّذِينَ (those who).
Form used for feminine superlative (meaning 'highest')
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's an alif maksura, which helps with pronunciation
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this word to the previous phrase 'those who are patient'
The doubled ب (shown by ّ) emphasizes the completeness of the nurturing and lordship
Adding kasra (ِ) shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else
Adding هِ shows it belongs to him. This word follows على (upon/on) in the ayah
Adding masculine plural possessive suffix to indicate multiple possessors
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense verb
Adding تَ at the start and doubling the middle letter shows you are putting complete trust in someone else (not yourself)
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at the start

steps.m: they put complete trust (in their Lord)
steps.n: The word changed from 'he trusts' to 'they trust' by adding ون at the end, showing multiple people are doing the action. It connects to رَبِّهِمْ (their Lord) to show who they trust.
This is a basic negative particle that can also mean 'what' in questions
Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before
Added fatha on م and lengthened the final alif for emphasis, but this doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the start makes the verb causative - emphasizing making something happen
Adding نا suffix changes the subject to 'we'. This is a complete verb form and doesn't have a case relationship with other words in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts from. In this ayah, it connects 'before you' (قَبْلِكَ) to 'We sent' (أَرْسَلْنَا), showing who was sent before.

steps.m: to face forward or come before

steps.n: Root letters ق-ب-ل became the noun 'before' by adding vowels fatHa and sukoon
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by what comes after it
Added ك at the end to show this means 'before you'. The word comes after مِن (from) in the ayah.
This is a basic particle that excludes something from what came before it. It's not derived from a root.
The shadda and alif are added to complete the particle. In this ayah, it's excluding 'men' (رِجَالًا) from those who were sent before.
The vowels make it into a proper noun meaning 'man'
Changes to broken plural form to show multiple men
Added ًا at the end to show these men are receiving the action of being sent (they are the direct object of أَرْسَلْنَا - we sent)

Changed from 'he' to 'we' as the subject
Added ى with small alif on top to show 'to/towards'
This is a basic preposition showing direction. Since it's a particle, we start with its simplest form rather than a root.
The ى changes to ي with a sukun when adding a suffix. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: to them (referring to the men mentioned as رِجَالًا in this ayah)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show who is being directed to. This refers back to the men (رِجَالًا) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added فَ at the beginning to show this action follows from what came before
Changed from singular 'he asked' to plural 'they asked' by adding وا at the end
Changed from past tense 'they asked' to a command 'ask!' for multiple people

steps.m: to settle, inhabit, be familiar with

steps.n: Added fatha and sukun to make the basic noun form. This word is what is being asked about in the command 'ask' (فَسْـَٔلُوٓا۟).
Added fatha at the end to show this is the direct object of the verb 'ask'
Added kasra and sukun to form the noun pattern that means 'the result or concept of the action'
Adding ال makes it specific. The ذ gets a shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: of the remembrance/reminder (possessed by أَهْلَ - people)

This is a conditional particle that introduces an 'if' statement. In this ayah, it connects to كُنتُمْ (you were) to mean 'if you were'
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed from 'he was' to 'you were' by dropping و and adding تُ at the end. The تُ shows it's 'you' in the past
Changed from 'I was' to 'you all were' by adding م at the end to show multiple people
This is a negative particle that makes the verb تَعْلَمُونَ (you know) into 'you do not know' in this ayah
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The ت at the start shows 'you' are doing the action now
Adding ون makes this plural 'you all' instead of singular 'you'
Connected the root letters with basic vowels
Doubling middle letter creates intensive meaning
Adding ات to the end makes it mean 'multiple clear signs/proofs' instead of just one clear thing
Adding ال to make the noun definite
The word gets the letter ب (meaning 'with') attached to its beginning, which slightly changes how it's written and pronounced, but keeps the same meaning of 'clear proofs'

steps.m: to write or inscribe (especially in stone)

steps.n: Root letters got damma vowels to make plural form meaning 'scriptures/writings'
Added ال to make it specific and shadda on ز because it comes after ال
Added و to connect this word to the previous phrase بِٱلْبَيِّنٰتِ (with clear proofs)
Changed ending vowel mark to match 'with clear proofs' (بِٱلْبَيِّنٰتِ) from earlier in verse. Small spelling change for smooth reading.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes someone cause the action instead of it happening by itself
Adding نَا shows 'we' did the action
Added و meaning 'and' at start and lengthened final alif. This word is connected to الذِّكْرَ which is what was sent down.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to pronouns

steps.m: to you (referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this means 'to you', specifically addressing Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in this verse
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: reminder/message (object of أَنزَلْنَآ - we sent down)
steps.n: Changed vowels to make it a noun meaning the result of reminding - this is what was sent down
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ذ letter because it comes after ال

steps.m: the reminder/message (object that was sent down)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this is what was sent down (object of أَنزَلْنَآ)

steps.m: to make something clear or distinct
to explain or clarify something thoroughly

Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb

Adding ت at the beginning and doubling the middle letter shows someone thinking carefully and deeply about something on their own

Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with'
Added هِ to show this refers to 'him/it'

steps.m: with them (referring to those who plotted evil)

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb

Added ي at start and changed vowels to show this happens in the present/future

Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Adding shadda (doubling) on ل shows the action is done multiple times
Adding تَ at start shows the person is doing the action to themselves
Changed verb into a noun showing the action itself

The small alif (ٰ) above the ى is just a pronunciation helper - it doesn't change the meaning. It shows that this ى is pronounced like a regular alif.

Added tanwin kasra to show this word is possessed by or connected to something else in the sentence

Added و at beginning to connect this word to previous words in the verse

This is not correct. 'Malaa'ika' (angels) comes from 'malak' (angel), not from 'malaka' (to possess). The plural form adds alif after first letter and changes middle vowels.

Connected the root letters with fatha to form the past tense verb

steps.m: he does/acts (subject of يَخَافُونَ - they fear)
steps.n: Changes from past tense (he did) to present tense (he does) by adding يَ at the start

steps.m: they do/act (subject of يَخَافُونَ - they fear)

steps.m: and they do/act (connected to يَخَافُونَ - they fear)

steps.n: Added fatha to show proper pronunciation. In this ayah, this word connects 'they are commanded' to what comes before it, showing what they are commanded to do.

The double damma (ٌ) at the end shows this is 'a god' rather than 'the god', like saying 'He is a god' instead of just 'god'

Changes from a verb (was alone) to describe someone who is alone/unique
Changed alif to alif with overbar (ٰ) and added tanween damma to show indefiniteness

The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

Added fatha to the base word ل which means 'to'

Added ي at beginning to show action happening now

steps.m: he hides himself (from مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ - from the people)
steps.n: Changed spelling to use alif with bar (ٰ) instead of regular alif. This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word ٱلْقَوْمِ (the people)
Added vowel marks and connected letters to form the noun meaning 'people' - those who stand together as a group
Added kasra to show this follows the word مِنَ (from)
Added ال to specify these particular people being hidden from
This is a basic preposition showing that someone is hiding from/away from the people
Changed the action into a noun meaning the evil thing itself
The ِ shows this word is connected to ما (meaning 'what') that comes after it. Together they mean 'what evil'
This is a relative pronoun that connects to بُشِّرَ (was given news of) in this ayah. It asks about or refers to the news/thing that was given.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Added shadda on middle letter to show intensity and positive meaning
Changed vowels to show this happened to someone rather than someone doing it
This is a basic preposition that shows how something is done
Added the pronoun هِ (him/it) to show who/what the preposition refers to

steps.m: with/by him/it (referring to the bad news he was given)
steps.n: Added ۦٓ which is a pronunciation mark that doesn't change the meaning. This word refers back to the bad news mentioned in the phrase 'مَا بُشِّرَ' (what he was given news of)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense form
Changes from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to ِ
The suffix 'هُ' means 'it' and shows that 'it' is the thing being held. The base word means 'he holds/grasps' and adding هُ tells us what is being held.
The أَ at the beginning turns this into a question. The ۥ mark is just a writing style for the هُ sound.
This is the basic form of the word that means something being high or elevated
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'

steps.m: upon (preposition showing something is on top of هُونٍ)
steps.n: The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is a long vowel sound. In this ayah, it shows something is upon/over هُونٍ (humiliation/disgrace)

steps.m: to be easy, light, or humble
steps.n: This root carries the basic meaning of being easy or light, which can extend to being humble or lowly
Changed the root letters into a noun by adding a 'u' sound (ُ) to mean the feeling or state of being humiliated
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) because this word comes after عَلَىٰ (upon) in the ayah, showing it's being acted upon

steps.m: or (connecting to أَيُمْسِكُهُۥ - should he keep it)
steps.n: This word أَمْ is used here to connect two questioning options. It links between 'should he keep it' (أَيُمْسِكُهُۥ) and 'should he bury it' (يَدُسُّهُۥ), showing someone trying to decide between two difficult choices.
Added pronoun suffix meaning 'it'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Added يَ at start to show present tense, changed vowels to damma, and combined repeated س with shadda
Added هُ at the end to show what is being buried/hidden
This is a basic preposition showing that something is being buried or hidden inside something else. In this ayah, it connects the verb يَدُسُّهُ (he buries it) with ٱلتُّرَابِ (the soil/dust).
Added vowels and alif to form the basic noun for the substance itself
Added ال (the) at the beginning, which causes the ت to be doubled (shown with ّ)
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after فِى (in) in the ayah, which requires the following word to take kasra

steps.m: now surely (introducing what يَحْكُمُونَ they judge)
steps.n: This is an attention-getting word that introduces an important statement. It's like saying 'Listen up!' or 'Pay attention!' before saying something important. In this ayah, it draws attention to how badly they judge.
Added basic vowel marks to connect the root letters
The weak letter و changes to ا, making the word shorter but keeping same meaning

steps.m: how evil/bad it became! (describing their judgment in يَحْكُمُونَ)
steps.n: The long آ adds emphasis to show strong disapproval of their judgment mentioned later in the verse
This word is connecting two parts of the sentence, referring back to what was announced. In this ayah, it's used to refer to the bad news that was given, not as a question word.
This root carries the basic meaning of judging or making decisions
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels to make it ongoing action
Added ونَ to show multiple people (men or mixed group) are doing the action
Added ال to make it specific. This is a special word that connects descriptions to what they're describing.
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many people, not just one person
Added لِ at beginning to show these people are receiving or experiencing something. The ل gets a shadda (ّ) when combined with ال.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it mean the opposite. Here it changes 'they believe' to 'they do not believe'
Added a fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. The hamza (ؤ) appears because it starts with أ.
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: to come after, to be delayed

steps.n: Changed to mean 'last/final' by adding أ at start and vowels - like saying 'more after' or 'most after'

steps.m: the life that comes after (this life)
steps.n: Adding ة makes this word specifically refer to the afterlife, which comes after our current life
Adding definite article makes it refer specifically to the afterlife

steps.m: in the afterlife (object of يُؤْمِنُونَ - they believe)
steps.n: Adding بِ shows this word connects to يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe), showing what they don't believe in
Added vowel marks to turn the root into a noun meaning 'example' or 'comparison'
Added the ُ ending to show this is the subject being described as evil in the phrase مَثَلُ السَّوْءِ (evil example)
Changed the action into a noun by adding vowel marks and making the middle letter silent
Makes the word definite by adding 'the' to it

steps.m: of the evil (possessed by/connected to something else)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word is connected to something else in the sentence
This is the base word referring to God in Arabic. It's a unique word that doesn't come from a typical root.
Added لِ at the beginning to show direction or ownership, and ِ at the end to show connection. The ا from ال is dropped when لِ is added.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it.

steps.m: to represent something by comparing it to something else

steps.n: Adding vowels turns the root into a noun meaning 'example'
Makes the word definite and shows it's the subject. In this ayah, it refers to the highest or supreme example which belongs to Allah.
The word means 'example' and ال makes it 'the example'
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added أ at beginning to make it a superlative form meaning 'highest/most high' instead of just 'high'
Added ال to make it specific and added the alif at the end to show it's a special form. This word describes Allah in this ayah.
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'he'. The ُ shows it's a subject and the َ helps with pronunciation.

Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to the previous phrase. This connects 'and He is the Mighty, the Wise' to the previous statement about 'the highest description belongs to Allah'.
Basic vowels (fatha) added to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he was strong'
Adding ي and changing middle vowel to kasra creates an intensive adjective showing a permanent quality
Adding ال makes it definite. This is one of Allah's names showing His absolute might and power.
Adding damma shows this is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: to judge wisely or make wise decisions

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the verb into an adjective describing someone who has wisdom
Added the definite article ال and the final damma to show this is describing Allah as one of His attributes in this ayah
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. This word introduces hypothetical scenarios that didn't actually happen.
Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement. The word introduces a hypothetical scenario related to the following verb يُؤَاخِذُ (he would seize/punish).
Added alif after first letter to show mutual action between two parties
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start. The middle alif became longer (ا). The meaning stays similar but now shows it's happening in the present.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject

steps.m: to be social, friendly with others

steps.n: The initial hamza is dropped when adding ال in the next step
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific people, and the ن gets a shadda

steps.m: the people (being acted upon by يُؤَاخِذُ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of يُؤَاخِذُ (He would seize/take to task)
Changed from action to the result of that action - like changing 'to wrong' into 'wrongdoing'
Added بِ to show this explains how/why Allah would take them to task (يُؤَاخِذُ)

steps.m: because of their wrongdoing (referring to ٱلنَّاسَ)
steps.n: Added هِم to show this wrongdoing belongs to 'the people' (ٱلنَّاسَ) mentioned earlier
This is the basic form of the word مَا which can mean 'what', 'that', or work as a negation particle depending on context. In this ayah, it's used for negation.
The shadda (ّ) is added to emphasize the negation. Here it's used with the verb تَرَكَ (left) to say 'He would not leave'.

steps.m: to leave something behind or abandon it
he left (referring to Allah in ٱللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word refers back to Allah (ٱللَّهُ) who is doing the leaving/sparing in this verse.
Basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on top of'
The ى changes to يْ before adding pronouns at the end
Added ها at the end to show this is happening upon/to something feminine (referring back to the word دَآبَّةٍ meaning 'creature' in this verse)
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects with the word دَآبَّةٍ (creature) to show 'any of the creatures' or 'from among the creatures'
The doubled middle letter shows this is happening with intensity or repeatedly
The ة makes this a noun referring to any creature that moves on earth
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's a long 'aa' sound
Added tanween kasra because it comes after مِن (from/of) in the ayah
This is a basic particle that shows contrast between ideas, like saying 'but' in English

steps.m: and but (connects this contrast with the previous statement)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this contrasting idea with what came before it

steps.m: to delay or put something after its usual time

steps.n: Doubling the middle root letter خ creates intensive meaning
Changes from past to present tense by adding يُ at the start

steps.m: he delays/is delaying them (referring to ٱلنَّاسَ - the people)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ to show the object (them) refers back to 'the people' mentioned earlier in the verse
Basic preposition showing direction or destination
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is just a spelling variation of ى. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the word. This is just a pronunciation mark that shows you should stretch the sound a little longer. It doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic noun meaning a set time or deadline
Added kasra tanwin at the end to show this is indefinite (like 'a/an' in English) and is being acted upon by the word إِلَىٰٓ (to)
Added مُ at start and doubled middle letter to show something receiving the action. The final و changed to ى because it's at the end.
Added tanween (double fatha) to show it's indefinite - meaning 'a' named time rather than 'the' named time
This is a conditional particle that introduces a future condition. In this ayah, it introduces what happens when their appointed time comes.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. Here it connects what happens when Allah delays people's punishment to what occurs when their time finally comes.
Added fatha vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'he'
Changed to hollow verb pattern with alif in middle
The alif is written with a madda (آ) to show it's a long vowel
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic noun form
Added damma to show this is the subject of 'came' (جَآءَ) in the ayah
Added هُمْ to show this appointed time belongs to them

This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ (they delay) into 'they do not delay'. In this ayah, it's part of explaining that when people's appointed time comes, they cannot delay it at all.

steps.m: to delay or move something to a later time

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding است at the beginning shows someone is trying to do something for themselves
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed to plural by adding ون, and hamza changed to match Uthmani script
The root letters were joined together and the middle letter و changed to ا to make the past tense verb 'he measured'

steps.m: a single unit of time, an hour
steps.n: Added ة to create a noun meaning 'one unit of time'
Added ً to show this is the object being acted upon in the phrase 'they cannot delay an hour'
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation of the negative particle
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this negation with the previous statement. This word negates the verb يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (they advance) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added است to show seeking or trying to do the action

steps.m: they seek to go forward (referring to people in general who try to rush their appointed time)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to people who try to rush their appointed time.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb form
Adding ي at the start shows the action is happening now
Adding ون changes subject from 'he' to 'they'
Adding و connects this clause to the previous one
Starting with the basic word for deity
Adding shadda on ل makes this the proper name for the one true God
Added لِ at beginning to show 'to/for' and ِ at end to connect it to the verb يَجْعَلُونَ (they attribute/assign)
This is a question word used to ask about things or connect parts of sentences. No morphological transformation needed as this is a primitive particle.
Added fatha mark to show correct pronunciation. This word connects to يَجْعَلُونَ (they attribute) as its object, showing what they attribute to Allah.
Added vowels to connect letters and show past tense meaning
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and adjusting vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: to describe or tell how something is

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added و (meaning 'and') at start + changed to present tense feminine form (she describes) to match tongues (ألسنتهم) as the subject
Root letters ل-س-ن become the noun 'tongue' by adding vowels
Changes from single 'tongue' to plural 'tongues' by breaking up the word pattern - a common way Arabic makes plurals
Changes from regular plural 'tongues' to plural subject form (with -u ending) to show these tongues are doing the action
The word 'tongues' gets 'their' added to it (هُمُ means 'their'), and it's doing the describing in the sentence
Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he lied'
Changed from 'he lied' (a verb) to 'the falsehood' (a noun) and added 'the' (ال) at the start
This is a basic particle that connects parts of a sentence, similar to how 'that' connects ideas in English
Adding shadda (doubling mark) makes the meaning stronger, emphasizing what comes after it in the sentence. Here it emphasizes that the Fire will be for them.
Added fatha to the preposition ل to show it's connected to what follows
Added ه (him) to show who it's for
Added م with damma to make it plural, showing it refers to multiple people. This word connects to مُّفْرَطُونَ (those who exceed bounds) in this ayah.
Changed the verb into a noun by adding damma and sukun
Adding ى to حُسْن makes it mean 'the most/best' (like turning 'good' into 'best')
Adds 'the' (ال) to make it specific, like changing 'best' to 'the best'
This is a basic negative particle that means 'no' or 'not'. It has no root since it's a primitive word. In this ayah, it combines with جَرَمَ to mean 'without doubt' or 'certainly'.

steps.m: no doubt (used with لا to emphasize certainty)
steps.n: This word evolved from meaning 'to cut off completely' to being used as an emphatic particle meaning 'without doubt' or 'certainly' when preceded by لا. In this ayah, it emphasizes the certainty of what follows.

steps.m: that (introduces a fact about لَهُمُ the good)
steps.n: This is a common particle that introduces a fact or statement. In this ayah, it introduces what their tongues falsely claim - that they will have good things.
Basic preposition meaning ownership or direction
Adds masculine plural pronoun to show who the preposition refers to
Added 'the' and emphasized the ن with a shadda
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb يَجْعَلُونَ (they make/assign)
Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before it
Added هُم at the end to show who the statement is about. This connects to the word مُّفْرَطُونَ (left behind/neglected) that comes after it.
Adds the third person masculine plural pronoun suffix

steps.m: to exceed limits or neglect something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added مُ prefix to make it about someone receiving the action, and changed the vowels to show it's being done to them rather than them doing it
Added ون to make it plural masculine
This is the base word referring to Allah, with shadda on the ل to show emphasis
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection
Added تَ at the beginning to create an oath formula, similar to saying "I swear by..."
Changed the alif to a special form (ٱ) used in the written Quran, but pronounced the same way
Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced. This particle shows something definitely happened or is complete.
Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis, like saying 'truly' or 'indeed' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the start makes the verb causative - emphasizing making something happen
Adding نا suffix changes the subject to 'we'
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. It connects what comes before it to what comes after it.
Added an alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the word which is a pronunciation mark showing that the sound is stretched slightly

steps.m: to be a source or origin for others

steps.n: Changes to plural noun showing multiple groups or communities
Added the two dots under the last letter to show this word connects to another word after it
Basic preposition showing origin or source
The 'n' sound is doubled because it blends with the end of the word before it
Root letters get vowels to make 'before' as a noun
Added kasra at the end because it follows the word مِن (from/of) which requires this ending
Adds كَ (meaning 'your') to the end to show it belongs to 'you' (when speaking to one male)
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Adding shadda makes the action stronger or more intensive

steps.m: and then he decorated extensively (subject: الشَّيْطٰنُ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this action happened as a result of what came before. The subject (doer) is Satan.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound
Added هُمُ to show who the action is for. This word connects to عَذَابٌ (punishment) in the ayah, showing who will receive the punishment.

steps.m: to be far away or rebellious

steps.n: Adding ي changes it from a basic 3-letter root to a 4-letter word meaning 'to act devilishly'

steps.m: a devil/satan (one who is extremely rebellious)
steps.n: Adding ا turns the word from 'acting like a devil' into 'one who is a devil' - like turning 'evil' into 'evil one'
Adding ٱل makes it refer to the specific devil/satan. The shadda on ش is because it comes after ٱل
Adding ُ shows this word is doing the action in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple deeds

steps.m: their deeds (referring to those whom the Shaytan deceived)
steps.n: Added the suffix هُمْ to show possession by multiple people, with alif changed to alif maqsurah (ٰ)
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'he' or 'it'. In this ayah, it refers back to Satan (الشيطان) mentioned earlier.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before - Satan beautified their deeds, so as a result, he became their ally.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and form the basic noun pattern
The doubled ي (shadda) intensifies the meaning to indicate someone who really protects/guards
Added damma to show this is the subject doing the action
Added هُمُ to show this is their protector
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make a basic noun. The fatha on يَ and sukun on وْ create the common noun pattern.
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific day rather than any day
The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of زَيَّنَ (he made attractive). In this ayah, it means specifically 'today' rather than just any day.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: and for them (connected to عَذَابٌ - punishment)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this is for 'them'. This word connects to عَذَابٌ (punishment) in the ayah, showing they will have a punishment.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (punished) to a noun (punishment) - like changing 'punished' to 'punishment' in English

steps.m: a punishment (indefinite, related to لَهُمْ - they have)
steps.n: The tanween (double damma) shows this is any punishment, not a specific one. This word is the subject of what they have.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (causing pain) to a word meaning something is very painful - like adding 'very' or 'intensely' to the meaning
Adding the double damma makes it indefinite - like saying 'a' instead of 'the'
This is the base particle that can mean either 'not' or 'what' depending on context. The alif is written long (آ) for emphasis.
Added و at the beginning to connect this idea with what came before. This combination introduces either a negation or question that relates to the next verb أَنزَلْنَا (We sent down).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

Adding hamza at the start makes the verb causative - instead of something coming down by itself, someone is making it come down. This is a verb, so it doesn't have nominative, genitive or accusative case.
Adding نا at the end changes 'he' to 'we' - this shows that multiple people (we) did the action
The preposition على changes its final ى to يْ when connecting to pronouns. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon you (referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ as the one receiving the Book)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ك which means 'you' (masculine singular). This word connects to أَنزَلْنَا (We sent down) to show who received the Book.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels

steps.m: book, writing (object of أَنزَلْنَا - We sent down)
steps.n: This form changes the verb into a noun representing the result of writing. The word is the direct object of أَنزَلْنَا (We sent down) in this ayah.

steps.m: the book (object of أَنزَلْنَا - We sent down)
steps.n: Adding ال (al) at the start makes it 'the book' instead of just 'book'

steps.m: the book (object of أَنزَلْنَا - We sent down)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb أَنزَلْنَا (We sent down)
This is a basic exception particle that limits or restricts what came before it. Not derived from a root.

steps.m: except (restricts what was mentioned in وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتٰبَ)
steps.n: The word 'illa' (إِلَّا) is the complete form of the word meaning 'except'. The shadda makes it a stronger word that clearly shows an exception is coming. Here it tells us the special reason why the Book was sent.
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
Shadda doubles the middle root letter to intensify meaning from 'be clear' to 'make clear'
Adding تُ at the start changes it from 'made clear' to 'you make clear' (like changing from past to present)
Adding لِ shows purpose - this word explains why the Book was sent down (as mentioned in أَنزَلْنَا earlier in the ayah)
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter lam
Added the basic singular masculine pronoun ه with damma to mean 'him'
Added م with damma to change from singular 'him' to plural 'them'

steps.m: the one who (that disagreed فِيهِ in it)
steps.n: Added ال at the beginning to make it more specific. The ل gets a shadda (ّ) because it combines with the following letter. This word refers back to what was disagreed about in the verse.
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters into a past-tense verb
The added letters show that people are disagreeing with each other - it's a back-and-forth action
The suffix وا indicates 'they (masculine)' as the subject of the verb
Basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'within'

steps.m: in it (referring to الْكِتٰبَ - the Book)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ه (him/it) to show what is being referred to. Here it refers back to the Book (الْكِتٰبَ) mentioned earlier in the ayah.

steps.m: to guide or show the right way

steps.n: Changed from basic root meaning 'to guide' into a noun meaning 'guidance' by adding damma (ُ) and tanwin (ً). The tanwin shows it's talking about guidance in general.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it in the ayah

steps.m: to show kindness and care for someone

steps.n: Changes verb 'show mercy' into the noun 'mercy'

steps.m: a specific act of kindness and care
steps.n: The ة at the end makes this refer to one specific instance of kindness/care
Added و (and) at the beginning to connect it with the previous word هُدًى (guidance). The ً at the end shows this is receiving the action.
The root meaning of standing/establishing becomes a noun for a group that stands together as a community
Adds لِّ (with emphasis) meaning 'for/to'
Adds tanween kasra to show this is indefinite (any people) and connects to the verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (who believe)
Adding alif at beginning makes it mean actively believing in something, rather than just feeling secure
Changes from 'believed' to 'believes' - adds يُ at start to show present tense
Adds masculine plural suffix to indicate multiple male subjects
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added shadda on ل to emphasize this is the one true God, not just any deity
Added damma at the end to show Allah is the subject doing the action in this sentence

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of أَنزَلَ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement. Allah is the one who sent down (أَنزَلَ) in this verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: He sent down (Allah sent down rain)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to show Allah is making something else come down (the rain). The middle letter gets a sukun (ْ) mark to help pronunciation.
This is a basic preposition that shows something coming from somewhere else or being part of something. It's like saying 'from' in English.
Added a fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word 'the sky'. This is like adding glue between words to make them flow better when speaking.

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated

steps.n: Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high up
Added ال (the) at the beginning which causes the س to get a shadda
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and lengthened the alif to آ
The root letters change to make the simple noun 'water' - like how we get 'sky' from س-م-و
The long آ makes the word stronger, like saying 'real water' or 'pure water'

steps.m: water (as the object being sent down)
steps.n: The ً (tanween fatha) shows this word is receiving the action of being sent down (أَنزَلَ) in this ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make it a past tense verb
Adding أ at the start changes the meaning from 'live' to 'make something live/give life to'. Like changing 'live' to 'bring to life'.

steps.m: and then he gave life to (the earth with water)
steps.n: Adds conjunction meaning 'and then'
This is a particle that shows what tool or means was used. Here it connects to the verb فَأَحْيَا (then He gave life) to show how Allah gave life to the earth.
Adding هِ refers back to the water (مَآءً) mentioned earlier in the ayah. It shows that Allah used water as the means to give life to the earth.
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to make it a noun

steps.m: the earth (object of أَحْيَا - brought to life)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to THE earth specifically. This earth is what Allah brought to life with water.

steps.m: the earth (object being brought to life by Allah)
steps.n: The َ (fatha) at the end shows that the earth is receiving the action of being brought to life
Added vowel marks to create the common preposition meaning 'after'. This word tells us when something happened in relation to مَوْتِهَآ (its death).
Root letters put together with fatha to make the basic noun meaning 'death'
Adding kasra because this word comes after بَعْدَ (after) in the ayah
Adding هَا with long alif (آ) at the end to show feminine possession. This refers to the earth (الأرض) which is feminine in Arabic.
This is a basic emphatic particle that strengthens statements. It's like adding 'truly' or 'indeed' at the start of a sentence in English.
