steps.n: Changes from 'he fixed/made right' to 'someone who fixes/makes right' by following the doer pattern فَاعِل

steps.m: a righteous deed (object of عَمِلَ - did)
steps.n: The alif is written as a long vowel mark (ٰ) above the ص. This word is what is being done (object) in the phrase 'did a righteous deed'
Added kasra to show this is a preposition that connects words

steps.m: from/of/among (describing ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ - male or female)
steps.n: Added shadda because this word follows a word ending in ن or tanween in speech, causing the sounds to merge. This is just a pronunciation change and doesn't affect meaning.
Adding vowel marks creates the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (remembered) to a noun (male) and added two dots under to show it follows the word 'from/of'
This is a conjunction that gives a choice between two options. Here it connects 'male' (ذَكَرٍ) with 'female' (أُنثَىٰ) to show the statement applies to either gender.
Changed from root letters to a noun by adding the vowel marks ُ (damma) and ْ (sukun) to mean 'feminine'
Added ى (alif maqsurah) at the end to make it mean 'a female' instead of just 'feminine'

steps.m: female (in contrast to ذَكَرٍ/male mentioned before)
steps.n: Added alif khanjariyah (ٰ) which is a writing variation that doesn't change meaning
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation: damma (ُ) on ه and fatha (َ) on و
Added the connecting particle وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

steps.m: one who believes/trusts (doer of the action)
steps.n: Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show who does the action. The hamza (ؤ) appears because it starts with أ.

steps.m: a believer (subject of وَهُوَ - and he is)
steps.n: Added tanween (double damma) to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a believer' instead of 'the believer'). This word comes after وَهُوَ (and he is) in the ayah.
Added نُ at beginning to show 'we' as the doer and added ِ (kasra) to show it's happening now
Added لَ to show this is a strong promise about the future

steps.m: so we will definitely give life
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this promise with the good deed mentioned before (عَمِلَ صٰلِحًا)

steps.m: so we will most definitely give life
steps.n: Added نَّ at the end to make the promise even stronger

steps.m: so we will most definitely give him life
steps.n: Added هُ to show who will receive this good life (referring back to مَنْ عَمِلَ صٰلِحًا - whoever does good)
Root letters ح-ي-ي became the verb 'to live' by adding the vowel marks
Changed from 'living' to 'life' by adding و and ة to make it a noun

steps.m: a life (object of فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ - we will give life to)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object receiving the action of giving in the phrase 'We will surely give him life'
The vowels show this describes a temporary quality
Added ة to make it feminine since it describes حَيَوٰةً (life) which is feminine

steps.m: a pleasant, pure, good (describing حَيَوٰةً - life)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha because it describes how the life is (acts like an adjective for حَيَوٰةً)

steps.m: to give someone what they deserve (good or bad)

steps.n: Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action

steps.m: certainly we give what is deserved
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to show strong emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'certainly'

steps.m: and certainly we give what is deserved
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this promise with the good life mentioned before

steps.m: and most certainly we will give what is deserved
steps.n: Added نَّ at the end to make the promise even stronger, like saying 'most definitely'

steps.m: and most certainly we will give them what they deserve (their reward mentioned in أَجْرَهُم)
steps.n: Added هُمْ (them) to show who will receive the reward. This refers to those who do good deeds mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: payment, compensation (object of نَجْزِي - we will reward)
steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun showing what is given as payment. This is what will be given as stated in the verb نَجْزِي (we will reward).

steps.m: payment, compensation (as the thing being given)
steps.n: Added a fatha at the end to show this is the thing being given in the sentence

steps.m: their reward (what We will give them)
steps.n: Added هُم at the end to show this reward belongs to them (the believers who do good deeds)
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Added أ at the beginning to show comparison

steps.m: with the best (of ما كانوا يعملون - what they used to do)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with' and ِ at end to show connection to what follows

steps.m: what (connects to يَعْمَلُونَ - they used to do)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'that which'. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَعْمَلُونَ to mean 'what they used to do'
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed to common past tense form with weak middle letter
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to يَعْمَلُونَ (they do/work) in the ayah, describing what they were doing.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start and changed vowels to show ongoing action

steps.m: they work/are working (referring to what they used to do in كَانُوا)
steps.n: Added ُونَ to show multiple people doing the action. This word describes what the people mentioned earlier used to do, as indicated by كَانُوا in the verse.
This is a word that shows when something happens. Here it introduces the time when you read the Quran.

steps.m: so when/then when (followed by قَرَأْتَ - you read)
steps.n: The فَ connects this with what came before, showing that what follows is a result or next step. In this ayah, it introduces what you should do when you read the Quran.

steps.m: to read or recite (especially sacred texts)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form. This word is connected to ٱلْقُرْءَانَ (the Quran) which is what is being read.
Adding تَ changes it from 'he read' to 'you read'. The sukun (ْ) is added before the تَ because two vowels can't come together.

steps.m: to read or recite something out loud
something that is meant to be read or recited
steps.n: Added special vowels and letters to change it from an action into a thing that receives that action. The ء helps separate the sounds.
Added ال at the beginning to show we're talking about THE specific Quran, not just any recitation

steps.m: the Quran (being acted upon by قَرَأْتَ - you read)
steps.n: Added a mark at the end to show the Quran is receiving the action of reading from the word قَرَأْتَ (you read) that comes before it
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he sought protection' to 'you seek protection!' by adding 'ist-' at the start and changing the ending to make it a command

steps.m: then seek protection! (command after reading Quran)
steps.n: Adds فَ to show this command comes after reading the Quran (mentioned in previous ayah)
This is the base form of Allah's name, with shadda on the ل to emphasize it
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection

steps.m: in/with/by Allah (object of فَٱسْتَعِذْ - seek refuge)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show how or by what means the action is done. In this ayah, it shows what to seek refuge in/by/with.
This is a preposition that shows something comes from or starts from something else. Here it connects to 'the Satan' (ٱلشَّيْطٰنِ) to show seeking protection from him.
The َ (fatha) is added to connect it smoothly to the next word ٱلشَّيْطٰنِ (the Satan). This is a common pattern when مِن comes before ال.
Added ي after first letter to make it mean 'one who is distant/rebellious' instead of just the action

steps.m: a devil (one who is extremely rebellious)
steps.n: Added ا to show intensity of the quality
Added ال to make it specific, which causes the ش to get a shadda because it's a sun letter

steps.m: of the devil (possessed by/connected to مِنَ)
steps.n: The word changed to show it belongs to/comes from the devil, with a small line under the last letter and a longer alif

steps.m: to cast out or drive away

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters into a basic past-tense verb
Changed from 'cast out' to mean 'someone who is cursed/cast out', like a title or label
Added ٱل to make it specific. The ر gets doubled (shadda) because it's a sun letter
Added kasra at the end because this word describes Satan (ٱلشَّيْطٰنِ) in the ayah
This is a word that makes what comes after it more certain. Here it emphasizes that Satan has no power.
The هُۥ at the end shows we're talking about 'he' or 'it' (referring to Satan in this case)
This is a special verb used for negation in Arabic. It's irregular and only appears in past tense form but gives present meaning.
Adding the basic vowel pattern to make it a usable verb
Negates the verb after it (يَتَوَكَّلُونَ) to mean 'they do not trust'
This is a preposition that shows direction or purpose. Here it connects with سُلْطٰنٌ (authority) to show who has the authority
The هُۥ shows this refers to الشيطان (Satan) mentioned earlier. This word connects with سُلْطٰنٌ to show Satan has no authority.

steps.m: to have power or control over something
steps.n: The root letters form a pattern for a noun meaning authority
Changed root letters into a noun meaning 'power' by adding the vowel marks ُ and ْ (damma and sukun)
Added ان at the end to show completeness of the quality. This word is the subject of لَيْسَ (is not) in the ayah.
Added tanween damma to show this is indefinite (any authority). The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this style.
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or has authority over something else. In this ayah, it connects to ٱلَّذِينَ (those who) to show who Satan has no authority over.
This is the basic form of the word used to connect descriptions to people or things
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
Adding ين makes it plural, changing from 'the one who' to 'those who'. This word describes the people who believed (ءَامَنُوا۟) in this ayah.
These three letters together give us the basic idea of trust and security

steps.m: to make someone feel secure, to cause to believe
steps.n: Adding the long alif at the start changes it from just 'feeling secure' to 'making others feel secure'
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together. The small circle (۟) helps with pronunciation.
Added fatha to connect 'and' to the next word
Added the preposition 'upon/on' which connects to the word رَبِّهِمْ (their Lord) in this ayah
Added the small alif (ٰ) to show the long 'aa' sound at the end
Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to show intensity, making it mean the ultimate nurturer/caretaker (Lord)
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it
Added هِمْ at the end to show this is their Lord
This is the basic concept of entrusting or relying upon something
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Added ت at beginning and doubled middle letter to show relying completely on someone else (not oneself)
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing ending to ُ - shows ongoing reliance
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
This is an emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Adding ما changes the meaning from emphasis to restriction, showing that what follows is the only possibility
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun meaning authority or power
Added damma to show this word is doing the action in the sentence that starts with إِنَّمَا

steps.m: his authority (as the doer of إِنَّمَا)
steps.n: Added هُۥ to show the authority belongs to him (referring to Satan in this context)

steps.m: upon/over (shows authority over الَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: This is a preposition showing that Shaytan's authority is over a specific group. In this ayah, it connects to الَّذِينَ (those who) to show who is under this authority.
Added ال with shadda to point to something specific, like saying 'the' in English

steps.m: those who (referring to the people who take Satan as an ally)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many people, not just one person

steps.m: to be close to or take charge of
he took charge of/became close to
steps.n: Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb
Added تَ at start and doubled ل to show doing action to oneself
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at the start - shows ongoing action instead of completed action
Changed to plural 'they' by adding ون

steps.m: they take him as an ally
steps.n: Added هُ to show who they take charge of
Starting with the base relative pronoun that refers to someone or something specific
Changes from singular to plural by adding ين at the end
Adds و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
This is the basic third person singular masculine pronoun

steps.m: they/them (referring to those who take Satan as an ally)
steps.n: Adding م changes the pronoun from singular (he) to plural (they). This refers back to those mentioned in the ayah who take Satan as their ally.
Changed the vowel to kasra (ِ) because it's being acted upon by something
Added ۦ after هِ to help with pronunciation when connecting words
Added the preposition بِ (with/by) to show how someone is affected by the action in يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُۥ (they take as allies)

steps.m: to join or associate things together

steps.n: Adding vowels makes this a complete past tense verb

steps.m: one who joins partners with Allah
steps.n: The مُ prefix and ِ vowel pattern change this into a noun describing someone who does this action regularly or as a characteristic

steps.m: those who join partners with Allah (plural)
steps.n: The ون ending makes this plural, showing it refers to multiple people who do this action
This is a time word that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it introduces 'when We substitute one verse in place of another'
The و connects this condition to what came before it, showing this is part of a larger discussion
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows this action happens with extra force or multiple times
Added sukun to prepare for adding 'we' ending
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action, with an alif to extend the sound

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be miraculous

steps.n: Adding ة at the end turns this into a noun meaning 'sign' or 'verse'
The words are actually the same - just written slightly differently. Both mean 'a sign/miracle'
Added tanween (ً) to show this is the object being replaced in the phrase 'when We replace a sign' (وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَآ ءَايَةً)

steps.m: to exist or be in a place

steps.n: Adding مَ at the start turns the idea of existing into a word for the place where something exists
Adding the fatha at the end makes it work in a past tense sentence

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident

steps.n: Adding ة at the end turns this into a noun meaning 'sign'
Adding tanween kasra (two dots below) shows this is indefinite and in genitive case
The word is spelled slightly differently but means the same thing - a sign/miracle. The ٍ (two dots under) shows it belongs to or connects with مَكَانَ
This is the base word for Allah, the proper name of God in Arabic. The doubled ل (lam) with shadda is a unique feature of this holy name.
The alif at the beginning changes to a special form called alif-wasla (ٱ) because it connects with the previous word.

steps.m: Allah (as the doer of أَعْلَمُ - knows best)
steps.n: The ُ (damma) at the end shows that Allah is the subject doing the action in this ayah - specifically, Allah is the one who 'knows best'.

steps.m: And Allah (as the doer of أَعْلَمُ - knows best)
steps.n: The وَ at the beginning means 'and', connecting this statement to what came before it.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added أَ at the beginning to show comparison, and added ُ at the end to show this is doing the action. This form is used to compare things, like saying something is 'more' or 'most' of a quality.
This is the first part of the compound particle, showing how or by what means something happens
Added مَا which means 'what' to ask about or refer to the thing being sent down. This word connects to يُنَزِّلُ (sending down) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the action more intense or repetitive, like sending down many things or sending down with force

steps.m: he sends down repeatedly (subject of the verb)
steps.n: Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at the start. The doubled middle letter ز shows this means 'sending down' repeatedly or gradually
Added fatha marks to make the past tense form. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: they said (referring to the disbelievers in this ayah)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show a group of people (they) did the action. In this ayah, 'they' refers to the disbelievers who were questioning the Prophet ﷺ.
Adding shadda makes this word stronger, changing it from 'if' to 'indeed'
Adding مَا changes the meaning from 'indeed' to 'only'. This is a special combination in Arabic where two words join to make a new meaning.
The alif is written with a long mark (آ) at the end of words in the Quran to show proper pronunciation, but doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: you (masculine singular, being addressed by قَالُوٓا۟)
steps.n: This is a pronoun that doesn't come from a root. In this ayah, it's being addressed by those who said (قَالُوٓا۟) something to the Prophet ﷺ.

steps.m: to make up or invent something false

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added اِ at start and ت after first letter to show doing action to/for oneself
Changed from a past tense verb (he invented) to a noun meaning 'someone who does the action' by adding مُ at start and changing the ending

steps.m: rather/but rather (contrasting with what was said before about being a fabricator)
steps.n: This is a particle that introduces a contrast with what came before. Here it's rejecting the claim that the Prophet was making things up (مُفْتَرٍ) and introducing the real reason - that most of them don't know.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
The أَ at the start and vowel changes make it mean 'more/most'
Added ُهُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to (them)
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَعْلَمُونَ (they know) into 'they do not know'. It's one of the most basic and common words in Arabic, used to say 'no' or make things negative.
Added a fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how it's pronounced with an 'a' sound
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
The يَ at the start shows it's happening now or in the future

steps.m: they know (referring to the disbelievers mentioned as أَكْثَرُهُمْ)
steps.n: Changed from 'he knows' to 'they know' by adding ون at the end to match with 'most of them' (أَكْثَرُهُمْ)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense form

This is a command to one person to speak. The middle weak letter و is dropped, and the vowels change to make it a command form. This word starts verse 16:102 and is followed by نَزَّلَهُۥ (he sent it down).
The doubled middle letter (ّ) shows this happened multiple times or with emphasis

steps.m: sent it down repeatedly (object of روحُ القُدُسِ)
steps.n: Added هُ (meaning 'it') to show what was sent down. This connects to روحُ القُدُسِ (the Holy Spirit) who did the sending down.

steps.m: sent it down repeatedly (object of روحُ القُدُسِ)
steps.n: Added a small mark (ۥ) after هُ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added damma on first letter to create the noun form. The و is part of the word's core structure.
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb نَزَّلَ (sent down).
Adding the vowel marks ُ changes it from a root into a noun meaning 'holiness'
Added ال (the) at start and kasra at end because this word describes رُوحُ (Spirit). Together they form رُوحُ ٱلْقُدُسِ meaning 'the Holy Spirit'

This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. In this ayah, it connects 'the Holy Spirit' (رُوحُ ٱلْقُدُسِ) to 'your Lord' (رَّبِّكَ), showing that the Holy Spirit came from your Lord.
Doubling the middle letter makes it stronger - like saying 'ultimate nurturer'
Adding kasra shows this word is possessed by or connected to something
Adding كَ shows the word belongs to you (singular). This word follows مِن (from) in the ayah.

steps.m: to be true, real, or right

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Combined the repeated letters with shadda to make a noun meaning 'truth'
Added the letter ب with kasra at the beginning to show how something is done
Added ال to show we're talking about 'the truth' specifically
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb نَزَّلَهُ (he sent it down)
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Adding shadda makes the meaning more intensive - from just being firm to actively making something firm
Changes to present/future tense and adds the subject 'he'

steps.m: so that he makes firm (purpose of نَزَّلَهُ/sent it down)
steps.n: Adds purpose meaning - shows this is why the Holy Spirit sent down the Quran
The base word ذا (this) changes to ذِي when used as a relative pronoun (who/which)
Adding ن at the end makes it refer to multiple people instead of just one
Adding ٱلْ with a shadda makes it mean 'those who'. This word connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (believed) to mean 'those who believed'
Added alif after first letter to show this is about actively doing the action of believing, not just the concept of safety
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did this action. This word is being affected by لِيُثَبِّتَ (to strengthen) in the ayah.
Added damma and fatha to change the verb into a noun meaning guidance
The word changes to end with ى (alif maqsurah) and gets two small dashes on top (tanwin) to show it means 'guidance' in a general way, not specific guidance
Added و to connect this word to the previous phrase about strengthening believers

steps.m: to give good news, to make happy with news

steps.n: Changed the word from an action (giving news) to the news itself by adding special vowels and ى at the end
Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this word with هُدًى (guidance) that came before it in the ayah

steps.m: to submit, surrender, be at peace

steps.n: The مُ prefix and vowel pattern shows who does the action of submitting. This creates the word for a Muslim - someone who submits to Allah.
The ين ending makes the word plural (more than one Muslim) and shows it's being affected by the word before it
Added ل prefix to indicate direction/purpose ('to/for')

steps.m: for the Muslims (receivers of هُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ - guidance and good news)
steps.n: Added ال to show we're talking about specific Muslims. In this verse, these Muslims are receiving guidance and good news.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of the base word قد which shows certainty
Added لَ at the beginning which adds emphasis, like saying 'surely' or 'definitely'
Added وَ at the beginning which means 'and', connecting this phrase to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense 'we' form. This word connects to what comes next in the ayah - what 'we know' is that 'they say...'
This is an emphatic particle that introduces and emphasizes what follows. It's connected to يَقُولُونَ (they say) in this ayah.

steps.m: that they (object of يَقُولُونَ - they say)
steps.n: Added the attached pronoun هُمْ (they/them) to show who is being talked about. This whole word is the object of يَقُولُونَ (they say) in this ayah.
يَ at start shows it's happening now and 'he' is doing it
The ُ (dhamma) shows this is a special type of verb with و in middle
The ending ُ shows this is in the normal (nominative) case
Adding ون changes it from singular (he) to plural (they)
Present tense verb meaning 'they say' - the ون ending shows it's a group of people doing the action
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger
Adding مَا changes the meaning from simple emphasis to restriction, like saying 'nothing but' or 'only'
Doubling the middle letter makes it mean 'to teach' instead of just 'to know'
Changes from past to present tense by adding يُ at the start - means 'he teaches' instead of 'taught'

steps.m: he teaches him (object of يَقُولُونَ - what they say)
steps.n: Added هُ (him) to show who is being taught. This whole phrase is what they (the disbelievers) are saying.
Added fathas to connect the letters and form the basic word for 'human being'
The double-damma (tanwin) at the end shows this is indefinite (any human being, not a specific one). This word is the subject of يُعَلِّمُ (teaches), telling us who is doing the teaching.
Forms noun pattern فِعَال commonly used for body parts and tools. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb يلحدون (they incline/refer to).
Adding shadda on ل adds emphasis to the word
Adding damma shows this word is doing the action. It is the subject of the verb يُلْحِدُونَ (they incline/refer to) in this ayah.
Added ى and ِ to create the specific demonstrative form
Added ٱلَّ at the beginning to transform it into a relative pronoun that connects parts of sentences

steps.m: to turn away from truth/right path

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يُ prefix and changing vowels
Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action. This word relates to the same 'they' as in يَقُولُونَ (they say) earlier in the verse.
Combines إِلَى (to) with هِ (him)

steps.m: to be unclear or foreign in speech/language

steps.n: Added أَ (a-) at the start to make it mean 'more/most foreign or unclear' compared to something else
Added kasra to connect with coming suffix that will show attribution
Added ي with shadda to show this describes something as having the quality of being foreign. This word describes لِسَانُ (tongue/speech) in this ayah.
Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite - referring to any foreign speech, not a specific one
Combines و (and) with هٰذَا (this)

Added ِ (kasra) on first letter, ا (alif) after second letter, and َ (fatha) before it to make the noun for 'tongue' or 'language'. This word is the subject of يُلْحِدُونَ (they incline to) in this ayah.
Added ٌ (double damma) at the end to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence

steps.m: to be clear/express clearly (like Arabic speech)

steps.n: Root letters connected with short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make the word for 'Arab'
Added ي with shadda and changed middle vowel to kasra to show something related to Arabs
Added two damma marks at the end to show it's any Arabic thing (not a specific one)

steps.m: making clear/distinct (describing this Arabic language mentioned in the ayah)
steps.n: Added مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show this describes something that makes things clear. In this ayah, it's describing the Arabic language (لِسَانٌ عَرَبِىٌّ) as being clear.
Added ٌ (tanween) at the end to show this is indefinite - meaning something that makes things clear in general, rather than referring to a specific thing

steps.m: clearly/distinctly making things clear (describing لِسَانٌ عَرَبِىٌّ - Arabic language)
steps.n: Added ّ on the م to emphasize how strongly this Arabic language makes things clear, contrasting with the foreign tongue (أَعْجَمِىٌّ) mentioned earlier in the ayah
This is a special emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger. It's like adding an exclamation point in English to show something is important.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who' or 'which' - it connects a description to something
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'

steps.m: those who (subject of لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - do not believe)
steps.n: Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people (masculine plural). This word is the subject of لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (do not believe) in this ayah.
This simple word changes positive statements into negative ones
Added fatha mark to show clear pronunciation
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. The hamza (ء) changes to (ؤ) because it comes after damma

steps.m: they believe/trust (in آيٰتِ ٱللَّهِ - Allah's signs)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word connects to 'Allah's signs' in the ayah, showing what they don't believe in

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident
a sign or miracle (something that shows truth)
steps.n: Root letters changed to make a noun meaning 'sign'. The long آ at the start and ة at the end make it a special noun for something that shows or proves.
Added ات to show there are many signs
Added بِ at the start to show these signs are being used as tools or means (like saying 'by means of the signs')
Added ِ at the end because this word connects to Allah (اللَّهِ) right after it, showing these are Allah's signs

steps.m: a deity, one who is worshipped
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ل with a shadda to show this is the one true God. The ِ at the end shows this word is being acted upon by something before it in the sentence.
This is a negative particle that makes something 'not' happen. In this ayah, it negates the verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (believe), making it mean 'do not believe'
Added fatha (َ) on ل to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'
Added vowels to connect the letters. The final ي changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added يَ at start to show present tense, with sukun and kasra vowels for present tense pattern. The ى returns to ي at the end.
Added هِمُ at the end to show 'them'. This word is what Allah (اللَّهُ) does - He guides them.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
Added ال to specify THE one God, doubled the ل with shadda, and added ُ to show this is the subject of يَهْدِي (guides) in this ayah
Added fatha to show basic pronunciation of the preposition ل
Added هُمْ at the end to show this applies to 'them' (masculine plural)
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Long ا after second root letter makes this a noun meaning the result of punishment. This word is the subject in the phrase ولهم عذاب which means 'and for them is punishment'.
Adding double dammah makes it indefinite, meaning 'a punishment' instead of 'the punishment'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
This form creates an intensive adjective showing something that causes a lot of pain
Adding the double damma makes it indefinite (like saying 'a' in English)
This is an emphasis particle that makes statements stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'truly' in English
Adding مَا changes the meaning from just emphasis to restriction, showing that something is limited or exclusive
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added letters (اِفْتَ) change the meaning from simple 'cutting' to deliberately making up or inventing something false
Changes from past tense 'he fabricated' to present tense 'he fabricates' by adding يَ at the start
Adding vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb
Verb 'he lied' becomes 'the lie' - now it's a noun that someone is making up
Object (thing being lied about) - connected to 'invents/fabricates'
This is a relative pronoun used to describe a group of people. Consists of definite article ال + ذي (who) + plural suffix ين
This is a negative particle that makes something 'not' happen
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added an alif at the start changed it from 'feeling safe' to 'having religious faith'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they believe (subject of لَا in لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word is negated by لَا (do not) in the ayah.

steps.m: a sign (something that shows or proves)
steps.n: Changed the root letters into a noun meaning 'sign'. The first two letters combine into a long آ sound.
Changed from one sign to many signs by adding ات
Added بِ at the beginning to show these signs are being used as proof or evidence
Added ِ to show these are specifically Allah's signs, connecting this word to اللَّهِ that comes after it
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Changed to the proper name of God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ِ shows this connects to the next word.
Added كَ at the end to complete the demonstrative pronoun. This word is the subject that هُمُ (they) refers back to in the next part of the ayah.
Adding وَ connects this to the previous statement about those who don't believe in Allah's signs
This is a basic pronoun that refers to a group of people (they/them)
The sukun (ْ) changes to a damma (ُ) because this word is connected to what comes after it. Here it shows that 'they' are the ones who are 'the liars' (الْكٰذِبُونَ).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he lied' to 'liar' - the doer of lying. Like changing from 'ran' to 'runner'. In this ayah it comes after 'they' (هم) to say 'they are liars'.
Added ال to point to specific liars mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: the liars (describes هم/they in هم الكاذبون/they are the liars)
steps.n: Added ون to show we're talking about multiple liars. The ُ shows this is the predicate (describes what هم/they are) in the phrase هم الكاذبون (they are the liars)
This is a word that introduces a conditional statement about people, connecting to the verb كَفَرَ (disbelieved) that follows it. It's like saying 'Whoever does something...' in English.

steps.m: he rejected/denied (truth) (connected to بِٱللَّهِ - against Allah)
steps.n: Added fatha after each letter to show this happened in the past. This word connects to بِٱللَّهِ which shows what was rejected.
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity/god
Added shadda on ل to make this word specific to Allah, the one true God
Added fatha on the shadda for pronunciation
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection
Added بِ at beginning to show how something is done, and added ٱ (alif with wasl) which helps connect the words in pronunciation
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects the verb كَفَرَ (disbelieved) with بَعْدِ (after) to show when the disbelief happened.
Added a small ن mark (called tanwin) that helps with pronunciation when connecting to the next word
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and form the basic noun meaning 'after'
Added kasra at the end to show this word connects to 'his faith' that comes after it
Connected the root letters together
Changed the word into a noun meaning the result of trusting - faith
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by or connected to something else
Added هِ at the end to show the faith belongs to him. This word comes after بَعْدِ (after) in the ayah, forming the phrase 'after his faith'

steps.m: except (connects مَن كَفَرَ to مَنْ أُكْرِهَ)
steps.n: This is a basic word that creates an exception - it connects the first part about 'whoever disbelieves' to the exception 'except whoever is forced'. Like saying 'everyone must do this EXCEPT those who can't'.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to whoever is forced (أُكْرِهَ) to say words of disbelief while their heart remains faithful.
Added vowels (fatha) to make it 'he forced' in the past tense

steps.m: he was forced (subject is the one who was forced to disbelieve)
steps.n: Changed to passive voice to show the person was forced by someone else, not doing the forcing themselves
Changed the root meaning from an action (to turn) into a noun (heart). The heart is called this in Arabic because it can turn emotions and thoughts over.
Added ُ to show this is doing something (the subject) and هُ to show it belongs to him. His heart is the thing that is at peace (مُطْمَئِنٌّ) with faith.
Adds 'and' to connect to previous phrase
Adding vowels to make the basic verb form
Added مُ at start to show 'one who' and changed inside vowels to show someone feeling peaceful (like changing from 'to calm' to 'calm person')
Added double damma to show this is any peaceful person (not a specific one)
Added a small stop mark to show where to pause when reading
Changes verb into noun meaning the act of believing
Makes the noun definite by adding 'the'

steps.m: with the faith (object that the heart is content with)
steps.n: Adds بِ meaning 'with' and ِ at the end because it follows a preposition. This shows what the heart (قَلْبُهُ) is content with.
Connected to مُطْمَئِنٌّۢ (content/at peace) - shows what the heart is at peace with. Has 'bi' (with) at start.
This is a basic particle used to express contrast or exception
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this contrasting statement with what came before
This is a basic interrogative/conditional particle that means 'who' or 'whoever'. In this ayah, it's used to mean 'whoever' since it's introducing a condition.
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to the word, making the meaning stronger. This emphasizes the importance of what's being said about 'whoever' does this action.

steps.m: to open wide, expand, make spacious
he opened wide (subject of صَدْرًا - his breast/heart)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is a completed action. In this ayah, this verb is connected to صَدْرًا (breast/heart) which shows what was opened wide.
Changed the verb into a noun by adding ُ (u-sound) on first letter and ْ (no vowel) on middle letter
Added ٱلْ (al) at the beginning to make it specific
Added بِ at beginning to show how the rejection is used or done

steps.m: by/with the rejection (of truth) (object of شَرَحَ - expanded)
steps.n: Added ِ at the end because it follows the word بِ. This word is connected to شَرَحَ (expanded) in the verse, showing what was expanded.

steps.m: chest (as the place that expands with feelings)
steps.n: Forms the noun for the place where expansion happens. In this ayah, it refers to willingly opening one's chest/heart to disbelief.
The -an ending shows this is the object (thing receiving the action) of 'expanded'. Like saying 'expanded his chest'
Starting with the basic preposition meaning 'upon'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement
Changed ى to يْ to prepare for adding the pronoun suffix
Added هِمْ at the end to show who is being referred to
Changes from verb 'to be angry' into the noun 'anger'
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is the subject. In this ayah, it means 'anger is upon them' (عَلَيْهِمْ).
Basic preposition showing source or origin
The 'n' sound blends into the next letter, shown by the shadda mark
Added fatha at the end to connect with the next word (اللَّهِ)
Added shadda to show that the ل is doubled for emphasis
Changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word, and added kasra at the end to show possession
Added fatha to the basic preposition ل to make it readable
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added هُمْ at the end to show who it's for (them)

steps.m: to punish severely or cause pain

steps.n: Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this verb into a noun meaning the result of punishment
Adding the double dammah makes this a general/indefinite punishment
Added vowels to make a past tense verb showing something becoming great
Changed the verb into an adjective by adding ي between the last two root letters
Added tanween dammah to make the word indefinite
Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is written as a small mark above the ذ to show extended distance
Adding لِ makes it point to something farther away

steps.m: that (pointing to something distant in meaning or time)
steps.n: Adding كَ completes the word, making it point to something distant in meaning or time. In this ayah, it points to the previous statement about disbelief.
Starting with the basic connecting word that joins two parts of a sentence. Here it connects to the next word ٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ (they preferred)
Adding بِ at the beginning changes 'that' to 'because' - it shows the reason for something
Adding هُمُ at the end shows who the word is talking about - in this case, it's talking about 'they'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show actively choosing or preferring something over something else
Combined the double letters with shadda to show intensity

steps.m: they strongly preferred (the worldly life over the hereafter)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people doing the action, and changed the first letter to ٱ
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he lived' (verb) to 'life' (noun) by adding و and ة at the end
Added the definite article ال. This word is what they preferred (object of اسْتَحَبُّوا - they preferred) in this ayah.

steps.m: the life (object of اسْتَحَبُّوا - they preferred)
steps.n: The word gets a fatha mark at the end because it's what they preferred (the thing being acted upon)
Changed the root into a feminine noun meaning 'lower' or 'nearer', often used to describe this life compared to the afterlife

steps.m: the worldly life (contrasted with ٱلْءَاخِرَةِ - the Hereafter)
steps.n: Added 'the' to make it specific. The ل combines with د making it doubled (shown by ّ). This word is being contrasted with 'the Hereafter' in this ayah.

steps.m: over (preferred the worldly life over ٱلْءَاخِرَةِ)
steps.n: This is a preposition showing that one thing is preferred over another. In this ayah, it shows the disbelievers preferred worldly life over the hereafter.
Added vowels to the root to make it mean 'last' or 'final' in a sequence
Added ة to make it feminine and specify it means 'the final life'
Added ال to make it specific and ِ to show it follows على (upon/over) in the verse. This word is being contrasted with worldly life (ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا) in this ayah.
This is a particle that introduces a new statement. Here it introduces what Allah does (لَا يَهْدِى)

steps.m: and that (connects to previous statement about بِأَنَّهُمُ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this statement to the previous one about why they preferred worldly life
Changed first letter to ل and added shadda to make it specifically refer to Allah, the One True God

steps.m: Allah (as the object being denied guidance to)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon in this sentence. The word appears in the phrase 'that Allah does not guide the disbelieving people'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it negative. Here it changes 'guides' to 'does not guide'.
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, changed middle vowel to sukun, and last vowel to kasra. This verb is negated by لا (not) in this ayah.
Added ى at the end to show it's a present tense verb. This is a common pattern for verbs ending in ي.
Changed root meaning from 'standing' to mean a group of people who stand together, making it a collective noun
Adding definite article makes the noun definite
Added fatha at end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb يَهْدِى (guides) - meaning Allah does not guide these people

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed to describe someone who does the action of rejecting truth
Added ين to show multiple people who reject truth

steps.m: the rejecters (of truth) (object of يَهْدِى - guides)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and fatha at end to show it's being acted upon. This word is the object that Allah does not guide in this verse.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'those'
Adding ئِ shows we're pointing to something far away

steps.m: those (far away, pointing to the people mentioned)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end makes it more specific about who or what we're pointing to. In this ayah, it's pointing to the people Allah sealed their hearts, hearing, and sight.
This is the basic singular form of the relative pronoun meaning 'who'
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people. This word is the subject of طَبَعَ (sealed) in this ayah, referring to those whose hearts Allah has sealed.

steps.m: he sealed (subject of a sentence about اللَّهُ sealing hearts)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. In this ayah, Allah is the one doing the sealing.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Adding 'the' makes it refer to a specific deity
Shows this word is the subject of the sentence
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or above something else. In this ayah, it connects to قُلُوبِهِمْ (their hearts) to show what was sealed.
Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show how to pronounce the word correctly with a long 'aa' sound
Adding vowels to root letters creates the noun meaning 'heart'
Changed the vowels and added و to make it plural (more than one heart)
Added هِمْ at the end to show these hearts belong to them
This form changes the action of hearing into the actual sense or ability to hear. In this ayah, it refers to the sense of hearing that Allah has sealed along with their hearts.

steps.m: their hearing (one of the senses Allah sealed)
steps.n: Added 'their' to show this refers to the hearing belonging to those whose hearts Allah sealed (أُو۟لٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ)
Added 'and' to connect this with 'their hearts' (قُلُوبِهِمْ) in the previous part, listing what Allah sealed
Changed the root into a noun meaning the ability or sense of sight
Changed from singular to plural by adding أ at the start and making the middle vowel longer (ٰ). This is a broken plural pattern.

steps.m: their sights (referring to قُلُوبِهِمْ وَسَمْعِهِمْ - their hearts and hearing)
steps.n: Added هِمْ to show these sights belong to the same people mentioned with the hearts and hearing in the ayah

steps.m: and their sights (connected to وَسَمْعِهِمْ - and their hearing)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this word with the previous mention of their hearing, showing these are things Allah has sealed

steps.m: those (pointing to the disbelievers mentioned before)
steps.n: This is a demonstrative pronoun used to point to things or people that are far away. In this ayah, it refers back to the disbelievers mentioned in previous verses.

steps.m: and those (pointing to the disbelievers mentioned before)
steps.n: Added 'and' at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This و shows that these people being pointed to have all the qualities mentioned in the previous phrases.
This is the basic form of the pronoun that means 'they' or 'them' when referring to a group of males or mixed group
Added ُ at the end because this pronoun is connected to the word after it (الْغٰفِلُونَ meaning 'the heedless ones'). It shows that 'they' are the ones being described as heedless.
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb showing a state of being
Changes from verb (being heedless) to noun (someone who is heedless)
Added plural ending to show multiple people
Added 'al' (the) at the start and 'oon' ending to show it's plural
This is a negative particle that makes things not true
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation. This word negates the verb جَرَمَ (doubt) that comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: to cut off, to commit (especially something wrong)

steps.n: When used with لا before it, this becomes an expression meaning 'certainly' or 'without doubt'. This meaning developed from the idea of 'cutting off' all doubt.
This is a connecting word that links two parts of a sentence. Here it connects to لَا جَرَمَ (no doubt) to say 'no doubt that...'
Added هُمْ at the end to show we're talking about 'they/them' (a group of people). The whole phrase now means 'no doubt that they...'
This is a basic word that shows something is inside or within something else
Added ى at the end to complete the word. This word connects to 'the Hereafter' to show where something is happening.
Changed root letters into a noun by adding alif at start and vowels to mean 'last/final'
Added feminine ending to create noun meaning 'the end/afterlife'
Added definite article to specify this particular end/afterlife
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the preposition في (in)
This is the basic form of the pronoun meaning 'they' when referring to a group of men
Added damma marks to show this is the subject pronoun 'they' that connects to the word الْخٰسِرُونَ (the losers). The second damma shows this is connected to what comes after it.
Changed to active participle form describing someone who performs the action. This word describes what the pronoun هُمُ (they) are.
Made plural to talk about many losers
Added 'the' to make it specific/definite
Basic form of the connecting word that shows one thing happens after another

steps.m: then, next, afterwards (shows sequence of events between جٰهَدُوا۟ and هَاجَرُوا۟)
steps.n: Added vowel marks (damma and fatha) and shadda to show how to say 'thumma' correctly. This word tells us one thing happened after another - like steps in order.
This is the basic form of the word that connects ideas together

steps.m: indeed/truly/certainly (adds emphasis to رَبَّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Adding the shadda makes the meaning stronger and more certain. This emphasizes the important statement about your Lord (رَبَّكَ) that follows.

steps.m: to care for, raise, nurture with complete attention

steps.n: The root letters r-b-b became the word 'Lord' with a fatha (َ) at the end because it comes after إِنَّ (indeed)

steps.m: your Lord (being acted upon by إِنَّ)
steps.n: Adds كَ to show this is your Lord. The whole phrase إِنَّ رَبَّكَ means 'indeed, your Lord'
This is the basic relative pronoun that connects descriptions to what they describe, like saying 'the person who ran' or 'the book which I read'
Changes from talking about one thing to talking about many things

steps.m: for those who (connects to هَاجَرُوا - emigrated)
steps.n: Adds 'for/to' at the beginning to show these people are receiving something or being affected by something. In this ayah, it connects to the word هَاجَرُوا (emigrated) to tell us about the people who emigrated.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
The alif makes it mean a complete journey/migration rather than just leaving

steps.m: they emigrated (referring to those who left Mecca for their faith)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from, starts from, or is made from something else. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
Added a small ن mark (called tanwin) that helps connect this word smoothly to the next word بَعْدِ (after). Together they mean 'from after'.
Added vowel marks to the root letters to make it mean 'after' instead of 'to follow'
Added ِ at the end to show this connects to the word ما (what) that comes after it, making the phrase 'after what'
This is a connecting word that links what happened (فُتِنُوا۟ - they were persecuted) to the previous phrase. It's like saying 'after what they were persecuted with'
Basic meaning of testing or trial
Changed to passive voice to show the action was done to someone rather than by someone

steps.m: they were put through trials/tests (subject of جٰهَدُوا۟)
steps.n: Added وا to show this happened to a group of people. These are the same people who later strived (جٰهَدُوا۟) in the path of Allah.

steps.m: then (connects this part about those who struggled to the previous part)
steps.n: This word connects two parts of the sentence, showing that one thing happened after another. In this ayah, it connects what happened to people after they faced trials with what was mentioned before.

steps.m: to put in effort or struggle
steps.n: Basic meaning of striving
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding alif shows this is about struggling against or with someone else
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. In this ayah, it refers to those who struggled against persecution after being tested.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Makes it plural masculine
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
Basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added shadda to strengthen the emphasis. This particle introduces and emphasizes the phrase about 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) that follows it.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, or develop something to its full potential
steps.n: Root meaning of nurturing/lordship
Adding fatha (َ) makes it a basic past tense verb

steps.m: Lord (with emphasis on complete authority and perfect nurturing)
steps.n: The double letter (shown by shadda) changes this from a verb (to nurture) into a special title meaning 'Lord' or 'Master'
Adding كَ shows possession (your). The word is in accusative case because it follows إِنَّ (indeed/verily), which makes its noun accusative.

This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the word هَاجَرُوا۟ (they emigrated) with بَعْدِ (after), showing they emigrated from/after something.
Added a small ن (called nun ghunna) at the end because it comes before the letter ب. This is just for pronunciation and doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a noun meaning 'after'. This word follows the preposition مِنۢ (from/after) in the ayah.
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by what comes after it in the phrase مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا (from after what)

steps.m: after it/her (referring to the trial/persecution mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added ها to refer back to the trial/persecution (فتنة) mentioned earlier in فُتِنُوا۟ (they were persecuted)
This pattern فَعُول is used for intensive qualities, showing someone who does something a lot
Adding لَ at start adds emphasis, like saying 'truly' or 'indeed'
Adding tanween makes the word indefinite, like saying 'a forgiver' instead of 'the forgiver'
Adding vowels makes it a complete past tense verb meaning to show mercy
Changed from a past-tense verb (he showed mercy) to a special form that shows someone who is always merciful by adding ي
Added shadda to emphasize the intensity of mercy
Added the -un ending (two damma marks) to show this describes Allah
Connected the root letters together to form the basic noun meaning 'day'
Added a fatha mark at the end to show this word is in the accusative case
Connected the root letters together

Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, and damma on last two letters
Added لَ at start to show emphasis and future tense. This word connects to بَيْنَهُمْ (between them) in the ayah.
Changes from verb root to noun meaning 'between'

steps.m: between them (referring to those who differed about the Sabbath)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which means 'them', referring back to those mentioned earlier who differed about the Sabbath
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic noun.

steps.m: on the day (when Allah will judge between them)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is expressing when something happens. Here it shows when Allah will judge between them about their differences.
Changed the و to ي and added ا to form a noun meaning the action itself
Added ة to show this is one specific instance or event
Added ٱلْ to specify this is THE resurrection (the Day of Judgment)

steps.m: of the resurrection (in يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيٰمَةِ - Day of Resurrection)
steps.n: Added ِ to show this word follows يَوْمَ (day) in the phrase 'Day of Resurrection'. The ا is written as ٰ in this form.
Starting with the two separate parts: في (in) and ما (what)
Combined the preposition في with the relative pronoun ما. In this ayah, it refers to the matters about which people differed regarding the Sabbath.
The preposition في combines with ما to ask about or refer to something within something
Added fatha after first letter and changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has و in middle). This shows past tense.
Added وا at the end to show this is about a group of people. This word is connected to يَخْتَلِفُونَ (they differ) in the next ayah, showing what they were doing.
Basic preposition showing location or containment. Here it's used with السَّبْتُ (the Sabbath) to show what they differed about
Added هِ at the end to show this refers back to السَّبْتُ (the Sabbath). The kasra (ِ) under ه shows it's connected to a single masculine word.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The added اِ and تَ show this is something people do with each other - they disagree together
Changed to present tense with يَ at start and different vowels
Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing this

steps.m: to invite or call (someone) to something

steps.n: The ُ at the end shows this is a normal present tense verb
Changed from 'I invite' to 'you invite!' by removing the 'u' sound at start. The alif helps pronounce the command form
The sukun and damma marks help show this is a command form
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects ٱدْعُ (invite/call) with سَبِيلِ (path) to show where to invite people to.
Added the small alif (ىٰ) at the end which is just a writing style - it doesn't change the meaning
Adding ي and vowels changes it from a verb into a noun meaning 'path'
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to 'your Lord' in the phrase 'path of your Lord'
The doubled middle letter (shadda) emphasizes the complete and constant nature of the nurturing, making it mean someone who nurtures completely and constantly
The kasra ending shows this word belongs to or is connected to سَبِيلِ (path/way) in the phrase 'path of your Lord'
Adding the possessive pronoun كَ to indicate 'your Lord'. This word is in the genitive case because it is possessed by سَبِيلِ (path/way) in the phrase 'path of your Lord'.
Changes the action of 'judging wisely' into the quality of 'wisdom' itself
Adds ة to show we're talking about a specific example or display of wisdom
Adds definiteness to the noun
Adds بِ meaning 'with/by' to show how the inviting should be done
Adds a kasra (small diagonal line) at the end because it comes after the word bi (with)

steps.m: to advise with wisdom and care

steps.n: Changed root letters into noun pattern with مَ at start and ة at end to mean 'one piece of advice'
Added the definite article ال to make the word definite

steps.m: the piece of advice (connected to الحكمة)
steps.n: The kasra shows this word is connected to الحكمة (wisdom) mentioned before it
Added 'and' (و) at the start to connect it with the previous word
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Adding ة makes it feminine and turns it into a noun meaning 'good deed'
ٱلْ makes it definite ('the') and the ِ ending shows it follows بِ from earlier
The added ا shows two people are debating with each other

steps.m: engage in reasoned debate! (command to one person)
steps.n: Changed from 'he debated' to 'you debate!' by dropping the first vowel and changing middle vowel to 'i' - makes it a command

steps.m: engage in reasoned debate with them (command)
steps.n: The suffix 'هُم' means 'them' and shows who the arguing is directed towards. It's an object pronoun, meaning it receives the action of the verb.

steps.m: and engage in reasoned debate with them (command in response to ٱدْعُ/invite)
steps.n: The prefix 'وَ' means 'and' and connects this command to the previous command ٱدْعُ (invite/call) in the ayah
This is the basic feminine relative pronoun used to connect clauses, like saying 'that' or 'which' when describing something feminine in English

steps.m: with that which (connects to هِىَ أَحْسَنُ - it is best)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show 'with/by/in'. This connects to the next phrase هِىَ أَحْسَنُ (it is best), telling HOW to debate - with that which is best.
This is a pronoun used to refer to feminine things or people. In this ayah, it refers back to الَّتِى (that which), describing the way or manner of debating mentioned earlier.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: better/best (than the previous way in جٰدِلْهُم)
steps.n: This form shows comparison, making something 'more' or 'most'. Here it's comparing this way of debating to other ways mentioned in the ayah.

Adding shadda (ّ) changes this from a conditional word into an emphasis word. It makes what comes after it stronger, like adding an exclamation mark in English. Here it emphasizes that 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) is the one who knows best.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters together
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and develops completely and perfectly
Adds ك to show it belongs to the person being spoken to. This word is affected by إِنَّ which comes before it in the verse.
This is a pronoun that refers to Allah (رَبَّكَ) in this ayah. It's the subject (doer) of the verb أَعْلَمُ (knows best).
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
This form shows Allah has this quality to the highest degree. It's connected to هُوَ (He) in the ayah, referring to Allah being most knowing of those who stray and those who are guided.
This is a question word used to ask about people or refer to someone. In this ayah, it refers to those who have gone astray.
Added the preposition بِ (about/with) to show what Allah knows about these people. This connects to the verb أَعْلَمُ (knows best) in the ayah.

steps.m: to stray from the right path

steps.n: Added fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he strayed'

steps.m: he strayed far (from the path of Allah)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shown by ّ) makes the meaning stronger, showing that someone has gone very far from the right path. This word appears in an ayah talking about who has strayed from Allah's path.
This is a preposition showing movement away from something. In this ayah, it connects the verb ضَلَّ (went astray) with سَبِيلِ (path) to show who strayed from His path.
Adding ي and vowels changes it from a verb into a noun meaning 'path'
Added kasra at the end because it follows the preposition عَن (from/away from)
Added هِۦ to show possession by Allah/Him
Added vowel marks to show this is a complete pronoun meaning 'he'. This word is the subject of أَعْلَمُ (most knowing) in this ayah.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Basic meaning of knowledge
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes to comparative/superlative form. This word describes هُوَ (He) in the ayah, saying that He is most knowledgeable.
Adding ا and ت changes the meaning to show someone finding guidance on their own, rather than guiding others. Like changing 'guide' to 'become guided'

steps.m: one who finds guidance (participle of اهتدي)
steps.n: The مُ at the beginning shows who does the action or who it happens to. Here it means 'someone who receives guidance'
Adding ين makes the word refer to many people, like changing 'a guided person' to 'guided people'
ال at the start makes it specific/definite

steps.m: with/by the guided ones (object of أَعْلَمُ)
steps.n: The ب at the start means 'with' or 'by'. This word connects to أَعْلَمُ (most knowing) in the ayah, showing who Allah knows best.
This is a compound particle made of 'wa' (and) + 'in' (if)

steps.m: to follow or come after something

steps.n: Added alif after first letter to show this is something done back and forth between people
Added sukun on ب to prepare for adding endings
Added تُمْ at the end to show that many people (you all) did this action in the past

steps.m: to follow with consequences or punishment

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show mutual action - something done in response to another action
Changed from past tense 'he punished' to present tense 'I punish' by changing the middle vowel from 'a' to 'i'
Adds 'then/so' to connect with previous statement

steps.m: then punish in return (all of you)
steps.n: Makes it a command for a group (you all)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make a noun meaning 'similar'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it

steps.m: with the like of (what you were afflicted with - مَا عُوقِبْتُم بِهِ)
steps.n: Added the preposition ب meaning 'with' at the beginning. This word relates to what follows in the ayah, referring to responding with treatment similar to how you were treated.
Basic form of the relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence, like saying 'that which' in English
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation. In this ayah, it connects 'with the like of' (بِمِثْلِ) to 'you were punished' (عُوقِبْتُم), meaning 'with the like of that which you were punished'

steps.m: to follow with consequences, punish in return

steps.n: This is the passive form of the verb, meaning the action is done *to* someone, not *by* someone.  It's like saying 'he was hit' instead of 'he hit'.
Added a sukun (ْ) at the end to connect with the next letter. The word means 'was punished' in both forms.
Added تم to show this happened to multiple people being addressed (you all)
Basic preposition meaning 'with'
Added هِ to show who/what the preposition refers to
Added small alif (ۦ) after هِ as a pronunciation guide
Starting with the basic conditional particle that shows something depends on something else
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. The إ changed to ئ because it comes between two vowels.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding تُ at the end changes it from 'he' to 'I'
The تم suffix indicates masculine plural 'you all' in past tense
This is the basic independent pronoun meaning 'he' or 'it', which can also function as 'is' in Arabic

steps.m: surely he/it is (emphasizing that being patient خَيْرٌ is better)
steps.n: The لَ adds emphasis to show certainty. In this ayah, it emphasizes that being patient is definitely better for those who are patient.
Changes the word into a noun meaning 'good/goodness'
Adds double dammah to show this is an indefinite noun in the nominative case
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show someone who has this quality
Added ين at the end to make it plural
Added لِ (li) at start to mean 'for/to'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he was patient' to 'be patient!' by adding اِ at start and changing vowels to make it a command to one person

steps.m: and be patient! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' at the start. This connects to the previous commands in the ayah. The alif changes to hamzat wasl (ٱ) after و.
This is a particle used here to negate what comes after it (your patience)
Adding وَ connects this phrase to what came before (وَٱصْبِرْ - and be patient)
Adding fatha and sukun to create the noun meaning 'patience'
Adding damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
Adding كَ at the end means 'your'. This shows who the patience belongs to. The whole phrase means 'your patience is only through Allah'
This is a basic particle that shows exception - it tells us that something is left out from a general statement. In this ayah, it's saying your patience comes from nowhere except Allah.
This is the base word - Allah's name. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this is being acted upon by the preposition that comes before it.
The بِ at the beginning means 'by' or 'with'. The ٱ is just a different way of writing the regular ال.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb تَحْزَنْ (be sad/grieve), telling the Prophet not to be sad
Adding وَ connects this phrase to the previous command وَٱصْبِرْ (and be patient), making this a second connected command
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by adding ت at start
Adding لَا makes it a negative command (don't do something)
This is a preposition showing something being above or on top of something else
Changed the ending to prepare for adding the pronoun
Added هِمْ at the end to show this means 'upon them' (referring to multiple people)
This is a negative particle that tells someone not to do something. Here it's telling the Prophet ﷺ not to be sad.

steps.m: and do not (negates تَحْزَنْ - be sad)
steps.n: The و connects this command with the previous command وَٱصْبِرْ (and be patient), showing these are related instructions
The و is a weak letter that affects how the word is formed
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it past tense 'he was'
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: you are/become (in a command or after لا)
steps.n: The و drops out and a sukun is added because it comes after لا (don't) in this ayah
The ن is dropped because this is a lighter way to say the word when it comes after لا (don't)
Basic preposition showing location or position inside something
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to ضَيْقٍ (difficulty/distress) in this ayah, showing what they are 'in'.
This root relates to things being tight or restricted
Changed from verb to noun meaning the state of being distressed. This word describes what you should not be in.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this means 'in any distress' and comes after the word في (in)
This is a preposition showing where something comes from or the source of something
Added ما which means 'what' to specify what we're talking about
The ن of مِنْ combines with the م of ما, creating a doubled م (shown by ّ). This is a common change in Arabic when these letters come together to make pronunciation easier.
Added يَ at beginning and changed first vowel to sukun to make present tense form
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: indeed/truly (emphasizes that اللَّهَ is with those who are mindful)
steps.n: This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. Here it emphasizes that Allah is definitely with those who are mindful.
Changed the root letters into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'

steps.m: Allah (the one true God being acted upon)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific, doubled the ل with shadda, and added fatha at the end to show Allah is being acted upon in this sentence. This is the proper name for the One True God in Islam.
This is a particle that shows Allah is together with or alongside those being described
The fatha marks on both letters show this word is being used as a location word (like saying 'along with' or 'together with')
Added ال to make it specific and point to a particular person or thing

steps.m: those who (referring to a group of people)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people, not just one person
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb. The ي changes to ى because it's at the end.
Added اِتَّ at the beginning to show doing the action to oneself. The ت combines with ق to make تّ.
Changed اِ to ٱ at start (common spelling variation). Added وا at end to show multiple people did the action.
Starting with the base demonstrative word that means 'the one who'
Changed from singular to plural by adding ين at the end
The alif at the beginning changes to a wasla form (ٱ) because it's in the middle of speech

steps.m: and those who (connected to مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added و with shadda at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this phrase to the previous statement about 'those who fear Allah'
This is the basic form meaning 'he' or 'it' when referring to a male or masculine thing

steps.m: they (referring to those who are mindful of Allah)
steps.n: Adding م changes 'he' to 'they'. In this ayah, it refers back to 'those who are mindful of Allah' (ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟)
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding مُ at the start and changing the middle vowels turns this verb into a noun meaning 'someone who does good deeds'
Adding ون makes this plural - now it means multiple people who do good deeds

steps.m: those who do much good (masculine plural)
steps.n: Doubling the م adds emphasis - means they do a lot of good deeds
